From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

City in the United Arab Emirates

Not to be confused with [Duba, Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Duba,_Saudi_Arabia "Duba, Saudi Arabia").

This article is about the city. For the emirate, see [Emirate of Dubai](/wiki/Emirate_of_Dubai "Emirate of Dubai"). For other uses, see [Dubai (disambiguation)](/wiki/Dubai_(disambiguation) "Dubai (disambiguation)").

**Dubai**[[a]](#cite_note-10) is the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates#Major_cities "List of cities in the United Arab Emirates") in the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") and the capital of the [Emirate of Dubai](/wiki/Emirate_of_Dubai "Emirate of Dubai").[[10]](#cite_note-dxbpopulation-11) It is on a [creek](/wiki/Dubai_Creek "Dubai Creek") on the southeastern coast of the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"). As of 2025, its population stands at 4 million,[[6]](#cite_note-DSC-6) 92% of whom are [expatriates](/wiki/Expatriates_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Expatriates in the United Arab Emirates").[[11]](#cite_note-:7-12) The wider [urban area](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") includes [Sharjah](/wiki/Sharjah "Sharjah") and has a population of 5 million people as of 2023,[[7]](#cite_note-Demographia-7) while the [Dubai–Sharjah–Ajman metropolitan area](/wiki/Dubai%E2%80%93Sharjah%E2%80%93Ajman_metropolitan_area "Dubai–Sharjah–Ajman metropolitan area") has a population of 6 million people.

Founded in the early 18th century as a [pearling](/wiki/Cultured_pearl "Cultured pearl") and fishing settlement, Dubai became a regional trade hub in the 20th century after declaring itself a [free port](/wiki/Free_port "Free port") (1901) and extending the Creek (1961).[[12]](#cite_note-:11-13) Modest oil revenue helped accelerate Dubai's development from the 1960s to the 1990s, when the city started to diversify [its economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Dubai "Economy of Dubai").[[12]](#cite_note-:11-13) In 2018, oil production contributed less than 1% to the emirate's GDP.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Rapid construction since the 1990s has produced one of the world's densest skylines,[[14]](#cite_note-15) including the [world's tallest building](/wiki/Tallest_building_in_the_world "Tallest building in the world"), the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa"). Extensive [land-reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") projects have added more than 300 kilometres (190 mi) of artificial coastline. The city has a large real estate market, especially in the luxury segment.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

Dubai's economy centres on trade, [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Dubai "Tourism in Dubai"), aviation, [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services"), and real estate.[[12]](#cite_note-:11-13)[[16]](#cite_note-17) The [Dubai International Financial Centre](/wiki/Dubai_International_Financial_Centre "Dubai International Financial Centre") (DIFC) is [one of the world's major financial centres](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index"). In 2024, Dubai was the seventh [most-visited city globally](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_international_visitors "List of cities by international visitors").[[17]](#cite_note-18) [Dubai International Airport](/wiki/Dubai_International_Airport "Dubai International Airport") (DXB) is the [world's busiest airport for international passenger traffic](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_international_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by international passenger traffic"), handling over 92 million passengers in 2024.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

## Etymology

Many theories have been proposed about the origin of the word "Dubai". One theory suggests the word used to be the *[souq](/wiki/Souq "Souq")* in Ba.[[19]](#cite_note-jundi-20) The linguist Zana Vahidzadeh (Dana Pishdar) holds that the word comes from 'money', a reference to the prosperity of the trading centre or that the word refers to 'two brothers'—Deira and Bur Dubai.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The poet and scholar Ahmad Mohammad Obaid traces it to the same word, but to its alternative meaning of "baby [locust](/wiki/Locust "Locust")" ([جراد](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D8%AC%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AF "wikt:جراد")) due to the abundance of locusts in the area before settlement.[[22]](#cite_note-UAEI-23)

## History

Main article: [History of Dubai](/wiki/History_of_Dubai "History of Dubai")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Dubai](/wiki/Timeline_of_Dubai "Timeline of Dubai").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Bronze_%26_Iron_Dagger%2C_Saruq_Al_Hadid.jpg/120px-Bronze_%26_Iron_Dagger%2C_Saruq_Al_Hadid.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bronze_%26_Iron_Dagger,_Saruq_Al_Hadid.jpg)

Bronze and iron alloy dagger, [Saruq Al Hadid](/wiki/Saruq_Al_Hadid "Saruq Al Hadid") archaeological site (1100 BC)

The history of human settlement in the area now defined as the United Arab Emirates is complex and extensive, formed by the extensive trading links between the civilisations of the [Indus Valley](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") and [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), and even by cultures as far afield as the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant").[[23]](#cite_note-24) Archaeological finds in the emirate of Dubai, particularly at [Al-Ashoosh](/wiki/Al-Ashoosh "Al-Ashoosh"), [Al Sufouh](/wiki/Al_Sufouh "Al Sufouh"), and the notably rich trove from [Saruq Al Hadid](/wiki/Saruq_Al_Hadid "Saruq Al Hadid")[[24]](#cite_note-:02-25) show settlement through the [Ubaid](/wiki/Ubaid_period "Ubaid period") and [Hafit](/wiki/Hafit_period "Hafit period") periods, the [Umm Al Nar](/wiki/Umm_al-Nar_culture "Umm al-Nar culture") and [Wadi Suq](/wiki/Wadi_Suq_culture "Wadi Suq culture") periods, and the three [Iron Ages](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") in the UAE. The area was known to the [Sumerians](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") as [Magan](/wiki/Magan_(civilization) "Magan (civilization)") and was a source of metallic goods, notably copper and bronze.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

The area was covered with sand about 5,000 years ago as the coast retreated inland, becoming part of the city's present coastline.[[26]](#cite_note-hist_trad-27) Pre-Islamic ceramics have been discovered from the 3rd and 4th centuries.[[27]](#cite_note-preislam-28) Before the introduction of Islam to the area, people in this region worshiped *[Bajir](/wiki/Bajir "Bajir")* (or *Bajar*).[[27]](#cite_note-preislam-28) After the spread of Islam in the region, the [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad "Umayyad") [Caliph](/wiki/Caliph "Caliph") of the eastern Islamic world conquered southeast [Arabia](/wiki/Arabia "Arabia") and drove out the [Sassanians](/wiki/Sassanians "Sassanians"). Excavations by the [Dubai Museum](/wiki/Dubai_Museum "Dubai Museum") in the region of *Al-Jumayra* ([Jumeirah](/wiki/Jumeirah "Jumeirah")) found several artefacts from the Umayyad period.[[28]](#cite_note-balbi-29)

An early mention of Dubai in 1095 is in the *Book of Geography* by the [Andalusian](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus")-[Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") [geographer](/wiki/Geographer "Geographer") [Abu Abdullah al-Bakri](/wiki/Abu_Abdullah_al-Bakri "Abu Abdullah al-Bakri").[[29]](#cite_note-30) The [Venetian](/wiki/Venice "Venice") pearl merchant [Gasparo Balbi](/wiki/Gasparo_Balbi "Gasparo Balbi") visited the area in 1580 and mentioned Dubai (as *Dibei*) for its [pearling](/wiki/Pearl_hunting "Pearl hunting") industry.[[28]](#cite_note-balbi-29)

### Establishment of modern Dubai

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Al_Fahidi-Fort.jpg/250px-Al_Fahidi-Fort.jpg)](/wiki/File:Al_Fahidi-Fort.jpg)

Al Fahidi fort in the 1950s

Dubai is thought to have been established as a pearling and fishing village in the early 18th century[[30]](#cite_note-:0-31) and was, by 1822, a town of some 700–800 members of the [Bani Yas](/wiki/Bani_Yas "Bani Yas") tribe and subject to the rule of [Sheikh Tahnun bin Shakhbut](/wiki/Tahnun_bin_Shakhbut_Al_Nahyan "Tahnun bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan") of [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Emirate_of_Abu_Dhabi "Emirate of Abu Dhabi").[[31]](#cite_note-32) In 1822, a British naval surveyor noted that Dubai was at that time populated by a thousand people living in an oval-shaped town surrounded by a mud wall, scattered with goats and camels. The main footpath out of the village led to a reedy creek, while another trailed off into the desert and merged into caravan routes.[[32]](#cite_note-:03-33): 17

In 1833, after tribal feuds, members of the [Al Bu Falasah](/wiki/House_of_Al_Falasi "House of Al Falasi") tribe seceded from Abu Dhabi and established themselves in Dubai. The exodus from Abu Dhabi was led by [Obeid bin Saeed](/wiki/Obeid_bin_Said_bin_Rashid "Obeid bin Said bin Rashid") and [Maktoum bin Butti](/wiki/Maktoum_bin_Butti_bin_Suhail "Maktoum bin Butti bin Suhail"), who became joint leaders of Dubai until Ubaid died in 1836, leaving Maktoum to establish the [Maktoum dynasty](/wiki/Al_Maktoum "Al Maktoum").[[30]](#cite_note-:0-31)

Dubai signed the [General Maritime Treaty of 1820](/wiki/General_Maritime_Treaty_of_1820 "General Maritime Treaty of 1820") with the [British government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") along with other [Trucial States](/wiki/Trucial_States "Trucial States"), following the [British campaign in 1819](/wiki/Persian_Gulf_campaign_of_1819 "Persian Gulf campaign of 1819") against [Ras Al Khaimah](/wiki/Ras_Al_Khaimah "Ras Al Khaimah"). This led to the 1853 [Perpetual Maritime Truce](/wiki/Perpetual_Maritime_Truce "Perpetual Maritime Truce"). Dubai—like its neighbours on the [Trucial Coast](/wiki/Trucial_States "Trucial States")—entered into an exclusivity agreement in which the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") took responsibility for the emirate's security [in 1892](/wiki/Trucial_States%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_Treaty_of_1892 "Trucial States–United Kingdom Treaty of 1892").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Dubai_Museum_and_Al_Fahidi_Fort.jpg/250px-Dubai_Museum_and_Al_Fahidi_Fort.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Museum_and_Al_Fahidi_Fort.jpg)

[Al Fahidi Fort](/wiki/Al_Fahidi_Fort "Al Fahidi Fort") houses the Dubai Museum.

In 1841, a [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") epidemic broke out in [Bur Dubai](/wiki/Bur_Dubai "Bur Dubai"), forcing residents to relocate east to [Deira](/wiki/Deira,_Dubai "Deira, Dubai").[[33]](#cite_note-hist_karim-34) In 1896, fire broke out in Dubai, a disastrous occurrence in a town where many family homes were still constructed from *barasti* (palm fronds). The conflagration consumed half of Bur Dubai's houses, and the Deira district was said to have been destroyed. The next year, more fires broke out. An enslaved woman was caught in the act of starting one such blaze and was put to death.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/A_Round_Watchtower_%28Name_Unknown%29.jpg/250px-A_Round_Watchtower_%28Name_Unknown%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Round_Watchtower_(Name_Unknown).jpg)

A watchtower in [Bur Dubai](/wiki/Bur_Dubai "Bur Dubai"), c. 19th century

In 1901, [Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum](/wiki/Maktoum_bin_Hasher_Al_Maktoum "Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum") established Dubai as a free port with no taxation on imports or exports, and also gave merchants parcels of land and guarantees of protection and tolerance. These policies led merchants not only to move directly from Lingeh, but also from those who had settled in [Ras Al Khaimah](/wiki/Ras_Al_Khaimah "Ras Al Khaimah") and [Sharjah](/wiki/Sharjah "Sharjah") (which had historical links with Lingeh through the [Al Qawasim](/wiki/Al-Qasimi "Al-Qasimi") tribe) to Dubai.[[35]](#cite_note-:72-36) An indicator of the growing importance of the port of Dubai is the movements of the steamer of the Bombay and Persia Steam Navigation Company, which from 1899 to 1901 paid five visits annually to Dubai. In 1902, the company's vessels made 21 visits to Dubai, and from 1904 on,[[36]](#cite_note-37) the steamers called fortnightly, trading 70,000 tons of cargo in 1906.[[37]](#cite_note-38) The frequency of these vessels only accelerated Dubai's role as an emerging port and trading hub of preference. Lorimer notes the transfer from Lingeh "bids fair to become complete and permanent",[[38]](#cite_note-:12-39) and also that the town had by 1906 supplanted Lingeh as the chief entrepôt of the Trucial States.[[39]](#cite_note-Lorimer_1915_454-40)

The "Great Storm" of 1908 struck the pearling boats of Dubai and the coastal emirates towards the end of the pearling season that year, resulting in the loss of a dozen boats and over 100 men. The disaster was a major setback for Dubai, with many families losing their breadwinners and merchants facing financial ruin. These losses came at a time when the tribes of the interior were also experiencing poverty. In a letter to the Sultan of [Muscat](/wiki/Muscat "Muscat") in 1911, Butti laments, "Misery and poverty are raging among them, with the result that they are struggling, looting and killing among themselves."[[40]](#cite_note-41)

In 1910, in the [Hyacinth incident](/wiki/The_Hyacinth_incident "The Hyacinth incident"), the town was bombarded by [HMS *Hyacinth*](/wiki/HMS_Hyacinth_(1898) "HMS Hyacinth (1898)"), with 37 people killed.

### Pre-oil Dubai

As well as expanding its regional trade links, Dubai was also an important regional centre for the collection, sale, and trade of pearls. The collapse of the pearling industry[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] plunged the city into a deep depression, and many residents lived in poverty or migrated to other parts of the Persian Gulf.[[26]](#cite_note-hist_trad-27)

In 1937, an oil exploration contract was signed, guaranteeing [royalty](/wiki/Royalty_payment "Royalty payment") rights for Dubai and concessionary payments to Sheikh [Saeed bin Maktoum](/wiki/Saeed_bin_Maktoum_bin_Hasher_Al_Maktoum "Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum"). But due to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), oil was not struck until 1966.[[32]](#cite_note-:03-33): 36–37

In its early days, Dubai was constantly at odds with [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Abu_Dhabi "Abu Dhabi"). In 1947, a border dispute between Dubai and Abu Dhabi on the northern sector of their border escalated into war.[[41]](#cite_note-dxbadconflict-42) Arbitration by the British government resulted in a cessation of hostilities.[[42]](#cite_note-rashasian-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Dubai_Creek_1964.jpg/250px-Dubai_Creek_1964.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Creek_1964.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/AlRas_Deira_Mid1960s.jpg/250px-AlRas_Deira_Mid1960s.jpg)](/wiki/File:AlRas_Deira_Mid1960s.jpg)

The [Al Ras](/wiki/Al_Ras,_Dubai "Al Ras, Dubai") district in [Deira](/wiki/Deira_(Dubai) "Deira (Dubai)") and Dubai Creek in the mid 1960s

Despite a lack of oil, Dubai's ruler from 1958, Sheikh [Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum](/wiki/Rashid_bin_Saeed_Al_Maktoum "Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum"), used revenue from trading activities to build infrastructure, initially through loans raised from local merchants and the ruler of Kuwait. Private companies were established to build and operate infrastructure, including electricity, telephone services, and both the ports and airport operators.[[43]](#cite_note-:4-44) An airport of sorts (a runway built on salt flats) was established in Dubai in the 1950s and in 1959, the emirate's first hotel, the Airlines Hotel, was constructed. This was followed by the Ambassador and Carlton Hotels in 1968.[[44]](#cite_note-:2-45)

Throughout the late 1950s, the British had urged Sheikh Rashid to approve a town plan to manage an already burgeoning real estate market. In 1959, through the correspondence of the British political agent, Sheikh Rashid invited architect John Harris to Dubai to discuss the parameters of a town plan. In May 1960, Harris returned to Dubai with the plan. Harris, who ran his own architectural practice, had only a matter of weeks to create the plan after receiving aerial photographs of the city that spring. No later than 1961, the British engineering firm [Halcrow](/wiki/Halcrow_Group "Halcrow Group") was marking the city's street system as shown on the plan and hardening designated routes with asphalt. The plan continued to function as a guide for further extensions of Dubai's street system as municipal funding became available. Harris's plan made only minimal proposals to disrupt the commercial and social life in the existing areas of Bur Dubai and Deira. Therefore, new development was proposed outside these areas, further inland from the Gulf. While the town plan of 1960 envisioned a very low-rise city that was not realised, its proposed road system was largely implemented as planned. Harris's firm acted independently of Halcrow, but the plan's accurate representation of ongoing land reclamation and the future Maktoum Bridge, which had not yet been proposed to the municipality, suggests that the two firms kept an open line of communication. The master plan's road system also served as a guide for the municipality in designating utility easements, as these became increasingly necessary.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)

In 1959, Dubai's first telephone company was established, with 51% owned by IAL (International Aeradio Ltd) and 49% by Sheikh Rashid and local businesspeople. In 1961, both the electricity and telephone companies rolled out operational networks.[[47]](#cite_note-:5-48) The water company (Rashid was chairman and majority shareholder) constructed a pipeline from wells at Awir and a series of storage tanks and, by 1968, Dubai had a reliable supply of piped water.[[47]](#cite_note-:5-48) The same year, a sand [tax](/wiki/Tax "Tax") was briefly imposed by Rashid in an effort to boost trade by monetising the Emirate's most abundant resource. The tax was lifted after 11 days, making it the shortest-lived tax in the region's history.[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)

On 7 April 1961, the Dubai-based MV *[Dara](/wiki/MV_Dara "MV Dara")*, a five-thousand-ton British-flagged vessel that plied the route between [Basra](/wiki/Basra "Basra") (Iraq), [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait"), and [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay") (Mumbai, India), was caught in unusually high winds off Dubai. Early the next morning, in heavy seas off Umm al-Quwain, an explosion tore out the second-class cabins and started fires. The captain gave the order to abandon ship, but two lifeboats capsized, and a second explosion occurred. A flotilla of small boats from Dubai, [Sharjah](/wiki/Sharjah "Sharjah"), [Ajman](/wiki/Ajman "Ajman"), and [Umm al-Quwain](/wiki/Umm_al-Quwain "Umm al-Quwain") picked up survivors, but 238 of the 819 persons on board were lost in the disaster.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

Construction of Dubai's first airport began on the northern edge of the town in 1959, and the terminal building opened for business in September 1960. The airport was initially serviced by [Gulf Aviation](/wiki/Gulf_Aviation "Gulf Aviation") (flying [Dakotas](/wiki/Douglas_C-47_Skytrain "Douglas C-47 Skytrain"), Herons, and [Viscounts](/wiki/Vickers_Viscount "Vickers Viscount")) but [Iran Air](/wiki/Iran_Air "Iran Air") commenced services to [Shiraz](/wiki/Shiraz "Shiraz") in 1961.[[47]](#cite_note-:5-48)

In 1962, the British [Political Agent](/wiki/Political_officer_(British_Empire) "Political officer (British Empire)") noted that "Many new houses and blocks of offices and flats are being built... the Ruler is determined, against advice [from the British authorities] to press on with the construction of a jet airport... More and more European and Arab firms are opening up, and the future looks bright."[[44]](#cite_note-:2-45)

In 1962, with expenditure on infrastructure projects already approaching levels some thought imprudent, [Sheikh Rashid](/wiki/Rashid_bin_Saeed_Al_Maktoum "Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum") approached his brother-in-law, [the Ruler of Qatar](/wiki/Ahmad_bin_Ali_Al_Thani "Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani"), for a loan to build the first bridge crossing [Dubai Creek](/wiki/Dubai_Creek "Dubai Creek"). This crossing was completed in May 1963 and was paid for by a toll levied on traffic from the Dubai side of the creek to [Deira](/wiki/Deira "Deira").[[43]](#cite_note-:4-44)

BOAC was initially reluctant to launch regular flights between [Bombay](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") and Dubai, fearing insufficient seat demand. However, by the time the asphalt runway of [Dubai Airport](/wiki/Dubai_Airport "Dubai Airport") was constructed in 1965, opening Dubai to both regional and long-haul traffic, several foreign airlines were competing for landing rights.[[43]](#cite_note-:4-44) In 1970, a new airport terminal building was constructed, which included Dubai's first [duty-free shops](/wiki/Duty-free_shop "Duty-free shop").[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Throughout the 1960s, Dubai was the centre of a lively [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold") trade, with 1968 imports of gold at some £56 million. This gold was, in the vast majority, re-exported – mainly to customers who took delivery in international waters off [India](/wiki/India "India"). The [import of gold to India](/wiki/Coinage_of_India "Coinage of India") had been banned, and so the trade was characterised as smuggling. However, Dubai's merchants were quick to point out that they were making legal gold deliveries and that it was up to the customer where they took it.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

In 1966, more gold was shipped from [London](/wiki/London "London") to Dubai than from almost anywhere else in the world (only [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") shipped more), totaling 4 million ounces. Dubai also took delivery of over $15 million worth of watches and over 5 million ounces of silver. The 1967 price of gold was $35 an ounce, but its market price in India was $68 an ounce – a healthy markup. Estimates at the time put the volume of gold imports from Dubai to India at around 75% of the total market.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

### Oil era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Dubai_Water_Canal_Business_Bay.jpg/250px-Dubai_Water_Canal_Business_Bay.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Water_Canal_Business_Bay.jpg)

View of [Business Bay](/wiki/Business_Bay "Business Bay")

After years of exploration following large finds in neighbouring [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Abu_Dhabi "Abu Dhabi"), oil was eventually discovered in territorial waters off Dubai in 1966, albeit in far smaller quantities. The first field was named "Fateh" or "good fortune". This accelerated Sheikh Rashid's infrastructure development plans and triggered a construction boom, resulting in a massive influx of foreign workers, primarily from Asia and the Middle East. Between 1968 and 1975, the city's population grew by over 300%.[[54]](#cite_note-pop7-55)

As part of the infrastructure for pumping and transporting oil from the Fateh field, located offshore of the Jebel Ali area of Dubai, two 500,000-gallon storage tanks were built, known locally as "Kazzans",[[55]](#cite_note-56) by welding them together on the beach and then digging them out and floating them to drop onto the seabed at the Fateh field. These were constructed by the [Chicago Bridge & Iron Company](/wiki/Chicago_Bridge_%26_Iron_Company "Chicago Bridge & Iron Company"), which gave the beach its local name (Chicago Beach), which was transferred to the Chicago Beach Hotel, which was demolished and replaced by the [Jumeirah Beach Hotel](/wiki/Jumeirah_Beach_Hotel "Jumeirah Beach Hotel") in the late 1990s. The Kazzans were an innovative oil storage solution which meant supertankers could moor offshore even in bad weather and avoided the need to pipe oil onshore from Fateh, which is some 60 miles out to sea.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Dubai had already embarked on a period of infrastructural development and expansion. Oil revenue, flowing from 1969 onwards, supported a period of growth, with Sheikh Rashid embarking on a policy of infrastructure development and economic diversification before the emirate's limited reserves were depleted. Oil accounted for 24% of GDP in 1990 but had fallen to 7% of GDP by 2004.[[57]](#cite_note-:1-58)

Critically, one of the first major projects Sheikh Rashid embarked upon when oil revenue began to flow was the construction of [Port Rashid](/wiki/Port_Rashid "Port Rashid"), a deep-water free port built by the British company Halcrow. Originally intended to be a four-berth port, it was extended to sixteen berths during construction.[[58]](#cite_note-59) The project was an outstanding success, with shipping queuing to access the new facilities. The port was inaugurated on 5 October 1972, although its berths were put to use as soon as they were built. Port Rashid was to be expanded in 1975 by 35 berths before the larger Jebel Ali port was constructed.[[57]](#cite_note-:1-58)

Port Rashid was the first of a swath of projects designed to create a modern trading infrastructure, including roads, bridges, schools, and hospitals.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

### Reaching the UAE's Act of Union

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Adi_with_the_three_rulers_of_Dubai.jpg/250px-Adi_with_the_three_rulers_of_Dubai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adi_with_the_three_rulers_of_Dubai.jpg)

[Adi Bitar](/wiki/Adi_Bitar "Adi Bitar") in a meeting with Sheiks [Rashid Al Maktoum](/wiki/Rashid_bin_Saeed_Al_Maktoum "Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum"), [Mohammad Al Maktoum](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum "Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum"), and [Maktoum Al Maktoum](/wiki/Maktoum_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum "Maktoum bin Rashid Al Maktoum") in Dubai, 1968

Dubai and the other "[Trucial States](/wiki/Trucial_States "Trucial States")" had long been a [British protectorate](/wiki/British_protectorate "British protectorate") where the British government took care of foreign policy and defence, as well as arbitrating between the rulers of the Eastern Gulf, the result of a treaty signed in 1892 named the "Exclusive Agreement". This was to change with Prime Minister [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson")'s announcement, on 16 January 1968, that all British troops were to be withdrawn from "East of Aden". The decision was to pitch the coastal emirates, together with [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar") and [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain"), into fevered negotiations to fill the political vacuum left by the British withdrawal.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

The principle of union was first agreed upon between the ruler of Abu Dhabi, Sheikh [Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan](/wiki/Zayed_bin_Sultan_Al_Nahyan "Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan"), and Sheikh Rashid of Dubai on 18 February 1968, at a meeting in an encampment at [Argoub Al Sedirah](/wiki/Argoub_Al_Sedirah "Argoub Al Sedirah"), near Al Semeih, a desert stop between the two emirates.[[61]](#cite_note-62) The two agreed to work towards bringing the other emirates, including Qatar and Bahrain, into the union. Over the next two years, negotiations and meetings of the rulers followed—often stormy—as a form of union was thrashed out. The nine-state union never recovered from the October 1969 meeting, when British intervention against aggressive actions by two of the Emirates prompted walkouts by Bahrain and Qatar. They dropped out of talks, leaving six of the seven "trucial" emirates to agree on a union on 18 July 1971.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

On 2 December 1971, Dubai, together with Abu Dhabi, [Sharjah](/wiki/Sharjah "Sharjah"), [Ajman](/wiki/Ajman "Ajman"), [Umm al-Quwain](/wiki/Umm_al-Quwain "Umm al-Quwain"), and [Fujairah](/wiki/Fujairah "Fujairah"), joined in the Act of Union to form the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"). The seventh emirate, Ras Al Khaimah, joined the UAE on 10 February 1972, following [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran")'s annexation of the RAK-claimed [Tunbs](/wiki/Tunbs "Tunbs") islands.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

In 1973, Dubai, along with the other emirates, adopted a uniform currency: the [UAE dirham](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_dirham "United Arab Emirates dirham").[[64]](#cite_note-britannica-65) In that same year, the prior [monetary union](/wiki/Monetary_union "Monetary union") with Qatar was dissolved, and the UAE dirham was introduced throughout the Emirates.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

### Modern Dubai

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Vereinigte_Arabische_Emirate_-_Dubai_-_Palm_Jumeirah_und_Dubai_Marina_-_im_Vordergrund_das_Hotel_Atlantis_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Vereinigte_Arabische_Emirate_-_Dubai_-_Palm_Jumeirah_und_Dubai_Marina_-_im_Vordergrund_das_Hotel_Atlantis_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vereinigte_Arabische_Emirate_-_Dubai_-_Palm_Jumeirah_und_Dubai_Marina_-_im_Vordergrund_das_Hotel_Atlantis_-_panoramio.jpg)

Dubai Palm Jumeirah and Marina in 2011

Throughout the 1970s, Dubai experienced continued growth fuelled by oil and trade revenues, even as the city witnessed an influx of immigrants fleeing the [Lebanese civil war](/wiki/Lebanese_civil_war "Lebanese civil war").[[66]](#cite_note-nyt2-67) Border disputes between the emirates persisted even after the formation of the UAE; it was only in 1979 that a formal compromise was reached, putting an end to disagreements.[[67]](#cite_note-lonelyplanet-68) In 1979, the establishment of the [Jebel Ali](/wiki/Jebel_Ali "Jebel Ali") port, a deep-water port accommodating larger ships, marked a significant development. Initially facing challenges, Sheikh Mohammed established JAFZA ([Jebel Ali Free Zone](/wiki/Jebel_Ali_Free_Zone "Jebel Ali Free Zone")) around the port in 1985, facilitating the unrestricted import of labour and the export of capital for foreign companies.[[68]](#cite_note-UAEFreeZones-69) Simultaneously, Dubai airport and the aviation industry continued their expansion.

The [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") in early 1991 had a negative financial impact on the city, as depositors and traders withdrew funds and halted trading. But Dubai rebounded in a changing political climate and prospered. In the late 1990s, various foreign trading communities—initially from [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait") during the Gulf War and later from [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain") amid the [Shia](/wiki/Shia "Shia") unrest—relocated to Dubai.[[69]](#cite_note-asianaffairs-70) Dubai served as a refuelling base for allied forces at the Jebel Ali Free Zone during the Gulf War and again during the [2003 Invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_Invasion_of_Iraq "2003 Invasion of Iraq"). Subsequent significant increases in [oil prices](/wiki/Price_of_oil "Price of oil") prompted Dubai to maintain its focus on free trade and tourism.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

In the early 2000s, construction of artificial islands off Dubai's coast, known as the [Palm Islands](/wiki/Palm_Islands "Palm Islands") and [The World Islands](/wiki/The_World_(archipelago) "The World (archipelago)"), began.[[71]](#cite_note-72) The [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa") opened in Dubai in 2010, surpassing the [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101 "Taipei 101") to become the [tallest skyscraper in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures "List of tallest structures").[[72]](#cite_note-73)

Dubai's smart city initiatives, including smart tourism, play a key role in advancing the city's growth ambitions, primarily through the Smart Dubai project.[[73]](#cite_note-74) In 2016, the world's first functioning 3D-printed office building was opened in Dubai,[[74]](#cite_note-Office_of_the_Future-75) having taken 17 days to build.[[74]](#cite_note-Office_of_the_Future-75) Its architect, [Killa Design](/wiki/Killa_Design "Killa Design"), was the same architect that designed the [Museum of the Future](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Future "Museum of the Future").[[75]](#cite_note-76) The construction of [Dubai Frame](/wiki/Dubai_Frame "Dubai Frame"), a 150-meter marvel, commenced in 2014, and was completed in 2017.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

During [heightened tensions in the Middle East in the 2020s](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_crisis_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Middle Eastern crisis (2023–present)"), Dubai's proximity to regional conflict zones led to frequent [flight cancellations](/wiki/Flight_cancellations "Flight cancellations") and heightened security concerns. In retaliation for [a series of joint Israeli-American strikes on Iran](/wiki/2026_Israeli%E2%80%93United_States_strikes_on_Iran "2026 Israeli–United States strikes on Iran") on 28 February 2026, parts of the city [were bombarded by Iranian strikes](/wiki/2026_Iranian_missile_strikes_on_the_United_Arab_Emirates "2026 Iranian missile strikes on the United Arab Emirates"), including the [Fairmont The Palm](/wiki/Fairmont_The_Palm "Fairmont The Palm") hotel and [the municipal airport](/wiki/Dubai_International_Airport "Dubai International Airport").[[77]](#cite_note-78)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Dubai](/wiki/Geography_of_Dubai "Geography of Dubai")

See also: [List of communities in Dubai](/wiki/List_of_communities_in_Dubai "List of communities in Dubai")

This time-lapse video shows the rate of Dubai's growth at one frame per year from 2000 through 2011. In the false-colour satellite images making up the video, the bare desert is tan, plant-covered land is red, water is black, and urban areas are silver.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Dune_bashing%2C_Dubai%2C_2007_%2808%29.JPG/250px-Dune_bashing%2C_Dubai%2C_2007_%2808%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dune_bashing,_Dubai,_2007_(08).JPG)

Dune bashing in one of the deserts of Dubai

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Dubai_by_Copernicus_Sentinel-2_in_false-colour.jpg/250px-Dubai_by_Copernicus_Sentinel-2_in_false-colour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_by_Copernicus_Sentinel-2_in_false-colour.jpg)

Dubai by Copernicus [Sentinel-2](/wiki/Sentinel-2 "Sentinel-2") satellite in false-colour in 2024

Dubai is on the Persian Gulf coast of the United Arab Emirates and roughly at sea level (16 m or 52 ft above). The emirate of Dubai borders Abu Dhabi to the south, [Sharjah](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sharjah "Emirate of Sharjah") to the northeast, and the [Sultanate of Oman](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Oman "Sultanate of Oman") to the southeast. [Hatta](/wiki/Hatta,_United_Arab_Emirates "Hatta, United Arab Emirates"), a minor [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") of the emirate, is surrounded on three sides by Oman and by the emirates of Ajman (in the west) and Ras Al Khaimah (in the north). The Persian Gulf borders the western coast of the emirate. Dubai is positioned at [25°16′11″N 55°18′34″E﻿ / ﻿25.2697°N 55.3095°E﻿ / 25.2697; 55.3095](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Dubai&params=25.2697_N_55.3095_E_) and has an area of 1,588 sq mi (4,110 km2), a significant expansion beyond its initial 1,500 sq mi (3,900 km2) designation due to [land reclamation from the sea](/wiki/Land_reclamation#Land_amounts_added "Land reclamation").[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80)

Dubai lies directly in the [Arabian Desert](/wiki/Arabian_Desert "Arabian Desert"). Still, its topography differs significantly from that of the southern UAE, with much of Dubai's landscape characterized by sandy desert patterns. In contrast, gravel deserts dominate much of the country's south.[[80]](#cite_note-geo1-81) The sand consists mostly of crushed shells and coral and is fine, clean, and white. East of the city, the salt-crusted coastal plains, known as *[sabkha](/wiki/Sabkha "Sabkha")*, give way to a north–south running line of dunes. Farther east, the dunes grow larger and are tinged red with [iron oxide](/wiki/Iron_oxide "Iron oxide").[[54]](#cite_note-pop7-55)

The flat sandy desert gives way to the Western [Hajar Mountains](/wiki/Hajar_Mountains "Hajar Mountains"), which run alongside Dubai's border with Oman at Hatta. The Western Hajar chain has an arid, jagged, and shattered landscape, whose mountains rise to about 1,300 metres (4,265 feet) in some places. Dubai has no natural rivers or oases, but it does have a natural inlet, [Dubai Creek](/wiki/Dubai_Creek "Dubai Creek"), which has been dredged to allow large vessels to pass. Dubai also has multiple gorges and waterholes dotting the base of the Western Al Hajar mountains. A vast sea of dunes covers much of southern Dubai and leads into the desert known as [The Empty Quarter](/wiki/The_Empty_Quarter "The Empty Quarter"). [Seismically](/wiki/Seismic "Seismic"), Dubai is in a very stable zone—the nearest seismic fault line, the Zagros Fault, is 200 kilometres (124 miles) from the UAE and unlikely to affect Dubai.[[81]](#cite_note-seismic-82) Experts also say the probability of a [tsunami](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") in the region is minimal because the Persian Gulf waters are not deep enough to trigger one.[[81]](#cite_note-seismic-82)

The sandy desert surrounding the city supports wild grasses and occasional date palms. Desert hyacinths grow in the *sabkha* plains east of the city. In contrast, acacia and [ghaf](/wiki/Prosopis_cineraria "Prosopis cineraria") trees grow in the flat plains within the proximity of the Western Al Hajar mountains. Several Indigenous trees, such as the date palm and [neem](/wiki/Neem "Neem"), as well as imported trees, such as the [eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus") and [jacaranda](/wiki/Jacaranda "Jacaranda"), grow in Dubai's natural parks. The [MacQueen's bustard](/wiki/MacQueen%27s_bustard "MacQueen's bustard"), [striped hyena](/wiki/Striped_hyena "Striped hyena"), [caracal](/wiki/Caracal "Caracal"), [desert fox](/wiki/Fennec_fox "Fennec fox"), falcon, and [Arabian oryx](/wiki/Arabian_oryx "Arabian oryx") are common in Dubai's desert. Dubai is on the migration path between Europe, Asia, and Africa, and more than 320 migratory bird species pass through it in spring and autumn. The waters of Dubai are home to more than 300 fish species, including the [hammour](/wiki/Hammour "Hammour"). Marine life off the coast of Dubai includes tropical fish, jellyfish, coral, [dugong](/wiki/Dugong "Dugong"), dolphins, whales, and sharks. Various types of turtles are also found in the area, including the [hawksbill](/wiki/Hawksbill "Hawksbill") turtle and [green turtle](/wiki/Green_turtle "Green turtle"), which are listed as endangered species.[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Dubai](/wiki/Climate_of_Dubai "Climate of Dubai")

Dubai features a [hot desert climate](/wiki/Hot_desert_climate "Hot desert climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") BWh). Summers are extremely hot, prolonged, windy, and humid, with an average high around 40 °C (104 °F) and overnight lows around 30 °C (86 °F) in the hottest month, August. Most days are sunny throughout the year. Winters are mild to warm, with an average high of 24 °C (75 °F) and overnight lows of 14 °C (57 °F) in January, the coolest month.

Dubai summers are also known for very high humidity, which can make them uncomfortable for many, with exceptionally high dew points that can exceed 30 °C (86 °F). [Heat index](/wiki/Heat_index "Heat index") values can reach over 60 °C (140 °F) at the height of summer.[[84]](#cite_note-85) The highest recorded temperature in Dubai is 49.0 °C (120.2 °F).

Very dry, Dubai's average annual precipitation is 79.2 mm (3.12 in). But precipitation has been increasing over the last few decades, with annual precipitation reaching 110.7 mm (4.36 in).[[85]](#cite_note-86) Record-setting [flooding](/wiki/Flooding "Flooding") inundated Dubai in April 2024, demonstrating a lack of appropriate [drainage](/wiki/Drainage "Drainage") to deal with the immense challenges precipitated by [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[86]](#cite_note-DubaiTorrentialFlooding-87)

## Government

Main articles: [Al Maktoum](/wiki/Al_Maktoum "Al Maktoum"), [Government of Dubai](/wiki/Government_of_Dubai "Government of Dubai"), [Dubai Municipality](/wiki/Dubai_Municipality "Dubai Municipality"), and [Politics of the United Arab Emirates](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Politics of the United Arab Emirates")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Mohammed_Bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum_at_the_World_Economic_Forum_Summit_on_the_Global_Agenda_2008_2.jpg/250px-Mohammed_Bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum_at_the_World_Economic_Forum_Summit_on_the_Global_Agenda_2008_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mohammed_Bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum_at_the_World_Economic_Forum_Summit_on_the_Global_Agenda_2008_2.jpg)

[Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum "Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum"), Prime Minister and Vice President of the United Arab Emirates and the Ruler of Dubai

Dubai has been ruled by the [Al Maktoum](/wiki/Al_Maktoum "Al Maktoum") family since 1833; the emirate is an [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"). Dubai citizens participate in the electoral college to vote representatives to the [Federal National Council](/wiki/Federal_National_Council "Federal National Council") of the UAE. The ruler, Sheikh [Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum "Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum"), is also the vice-president and [Prime Minister of the United Arab Emirates](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Prime Minister of the United Arab Emirates") and a member of the Supreme Council of the Union (SCU). Dubai appoints eight members to the [Federal National Council](/wiki/Federal_National_Council "Federal National Council") (FNC) of the UAE, the supreme federal legislative body, for two-term periods.[[92]](#cite_note-uaegovt-93)

The [Dubai Municipality](/wiki/Dubai_Municipality "Dubai Municipality") (DM) was established by the then ruler of Dubai, [Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum](/wiki/Rashid_bin_Saeed_Al_Maktoum "Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum"), in 1954 for purposes of city planning, citizen services, and upkeep of local facilities.[[93]](#cite_note-dmhistory-94) It has since then evolved into an autonomous subnational authority, collectively known as the [Government of Dubai](/wiki/Government_of_Dubai "Government of Dubai"), which is responsible for both the city of Dubai and the greater emirate.[[94]](#cite_note-95) The Government of Dubai has over 58 governmental departments responsible for security, economic policy, education, transportation, immigration, and is one of only three emirates to have a separate judicial system independent from the federal judiciary of the UAE.[[95]](#cite_note-96) The [Ruler of Dubai](/wiki/Ruler_of_Dubai "Ruler of Dubai") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") and [emir](/wiki/Emir "Emir") (head of state) and laws, decrees, and court judgments are issued in his name, however, since 2003, executive authority of managing and overseeing Dubai Governmental agencies has been delegated to the [Dubai Executive Council](/wiki/Government_of_Dubai#Dubai_Executive_Council "Government of Dubai"), led by the [Crown Prince of Dubai](/wiki/Crown_prince_of_Dubai "Crown prince of Dubai") [Hamdan bin Mohammed Al Maktoum](/wiki/Hamdan_bin_Mohammed_Al_Maktoum "Hamdan bin Mohammed Al Maktoum"). Although no legislative assembly exists, the traditional [open majlis](/wiki/Majlis "Majlis") (council), where citizens and representatives of the Ruler meet, is often used for feedback on certain domestic issues.[[96]](#cite_note-97)[[97]](#cite_note-98)

### Ethnicity and languages

See also: [Emirati people](/wiki/Emirati_people "Emirati people") and [Expatriates in the United Arab Emirates](/wiki/Expatriates_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Expatriates in the United Arab Emirates")

As of September 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dubai&action=edit), the population is 3,331,420 –an annual increase of 177,020 people, which represents a growth rate of 5.64%.[[107]](#cite_note-dsc.gov.ae-108) The region covers 1,287.5 square kilometres (497.1 mi2). The population density is 408.18/km2 –more than eight times that of the entire country. Dubai is the  [second-most-expensive city](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_cities_for_expatriate_employees "List of most expensive cities for expatriate employees") in the region and the 20th-most-expensive city in the world.[[108]](#cite_note-109)

As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dubai&action=edit), only about 15% of the emirate's population was made up of [UAE nationals](/wiki/Emirati_people "Emirati people"),[[109]](#cite_note-110) with the rest comprising expatriates, many of whom either have been in the country for generations or were born in the UAE.[[110]](#cite_note-111)[[111]](#cite_note-112) Approximately 85% of the [expatriate population](/wiki/Expatriates_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Expatriates in the United Arab Emirates") (and 71% of the emirate's total population) was Asian, chiefly [Indian](/wiki/Indians_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Indians in the United Arab Emirates") (51%) and [Pakistani](/wiki/Pakistanis_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Pakistanis in the United Arab Emirates") (16%); other significant Asian groups include [Bangladeshis](/wiki/Bangladeshis_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Bangladeshis in the United Arab Emirates") (9%) and [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipinos_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Filipinos in the United Arab Emirates") (3%).[[112]](#cite_note-migrationinformation-113) A quarter of the population (local and foreign) reportedly [traces their origins to Iran](/wiki/Iranians_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Iranians in the United Arab Emirates").[[113]](#cite_note-114) In addition, 16% of the population (or 288,000 persons) [living in collective labour](/wiki/Labour_force_of_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Labour force of the United Arab Emirates") accommodation were not identified by ethnicity or nationality, but were thought to be primarily Asian.[[114]](#cite_note-pop4-115) Some 461,000 [Westerners](/wiki/Westerners "Westerners") live in the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"), making up 5.1% of its total population.[[115]](#cite_note-web.archive.org-116)[[116]](#cite_note-117) There are over 100,000 British expatriates in Dubai, by far the largest group of Western expatriates in the city.[[117]](#cite_note-118) The median age in the emirate was about 27 years. In 2014, there were estimated to be 15.54 [births](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") and 1.99 [deaths](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate") per 1,000 people.[[118]](#cite_note-119) There are other Arab nationals, including [GCC](/wiki/Gulf_Cooperation_Council "Gulf Cooperation Council") nationals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") is the national and official language of the UAE. The [Gulf dialect](/wiki/Gulf_Arabic "Gulf Arabic") of Arabic is spoken natively by most Emiratis; some Emiratis also speak [Shihhi Arabic](/wiki/Shihhi_Arabic "Shihhi Arabic").[[119]](#cite_note-120) [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is used as a second language. Other major languages spoken in Dubai due to immigration are [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language"), [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language"), [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_language "Hindi language"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [Balochi](/wiki/Balochi_language "Balochi language"), [Tulu](/wiki/Tulu_language "Tulu language"),[[120]](#cite_note-121) [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada"), [Sinhala](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language"), [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language"), [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), and [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), in addition to many others.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

### Religion

See also: [Religion in the United Arab Emirates](/wiki/Religion_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Religion in the United Arab Emirates")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Al_Salam_Mosque_at_night.jpg/250px-Al_Salam_Mosque_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Al_Salam_Mosque_at_night.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Saint_Mary%27s_Catholic_Church_2025.jpg/250px-Saint_Mary%27s_Catholic_Church_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Mary%27s_Catholic_Church_2025.jpg)

Al Salam Mosque in [Al Barsha](/wiki/Al_Barsha "Al Barsha") and [St. Mary's Catholic Church, Dubai](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Catholic_Church,_Dubai "St. Mary's Catholic Church, Dubai")

Article 7 of the UAE's Provisional Constitution declares [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") the official state religion of the UAE. The government subsidises almost 95% of mosques and employs all [Imams](/wiki/Imam "Imam"); approximately 5% of mosques are entirely private, and several large mosques have large private endowments.[[122]](#cite_note-loc_religion-123) All mosques in Dubai are managed by the [Islamic Affairs and Charitable Activities Department](/wiki/Islamic_Affairs_and_Charitable_Activities_Department "Islamic Affairs and Charitable Activities Department"), also known as "Awqaf", under the Government of Dubai. The Government appoints all [Imams](/wiki/Imam "Imam").[[123]](#cite_note-124) The Constitution of the United Arab Emirates provides for [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion"). Expats held to be preaching [religious hatred](/wiki/Religious_intolerance "Religious intolerance") or promoting [religious extremism](/wiki/Religious_extremism "Religious extremism") are usually jailed and deported.[[124]](#cite_note-125)

| Others | |  | 2% | |

Dubai has large [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians"), [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu"), [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh"), [Baháʼí](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"), [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist"), [Jain](/wiki/Jain "Jain") and other religious communities residing in the city, as well as a small but growing [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") community.[[126]](#cite_note-127) In 2014, more than 56% of Dubai residents were Muslims, while 25% were Christians, 16% were Hindus, and around 2% were adherent of other religions.[[125]](#cite_note-RELIGION-126) The [Churches Complex](/wiki/Churches_Complex "Churches Complex") in [Jebel Ali Village](/wiki/Jebel_Ali_Village "Jebel Ali Village") is an area for several churches and temples of different [religious denominations](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination"), especially [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination").[[127]](#cite_note-thingstodopost-128) The largest Christian parish in the world is that of [St Mary's Catholic Church](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Catholic_Church,_Dubai "St. Mary's Catholic Church, Dubai") in Dubai, owing to the large number of migrant workers in the city.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

Non-Muslim groups may own houses of worship where they can practice their religion freely, provided they obtain a land grant and permission to build a compound. Groups that do not have their own buildings may use facilities provided by other religious organisations or worship in private homes.[[129]](#cite_note-130) Non-Muslim religious groups are also permitted to advertise group functions openly and distribute various religious literature. Catholics are served pastorally by the [Apostolic Vicariate of Southern Arabia](/wiki/Apostolic_Vicariate_of_Southern_Arabia "Apostolic Vicariate of Southern Arabia"). British preacher [Reverend Andrew Thompson](/wiki/Andrew_Thompson_(priest) "Andrew Thompson (priest)") claimed that the United Arab Emirates is one of the most tolerant places in the world towards Christians and that it is easier to be a Christian in the UAE than in the UK.[[130]](#cite_note-131) On 5 April 2020, the [Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") announced the building of one of their temples in Dubai. As part of the announcement, church President Russell M. Nelson said, "The plan for a temple in Dubai comes in response to their gracious invitation, which we gratefully acknowledge."[[131]](#cite_note-132)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Dubai](/wiki/Economy_of_Dubai "Economy of Dubai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c7/Burj_Khalifa_2021.jpg/250px-Burj_Khalifa_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burj_Khalifa_2021.jpg)

[Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa") and [Downtown Dubai](/wiki/Downtown_Dubai "Downtown Dubai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Dubai_Marina_04.jpg/250px-Dubai_Marina_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Marina_04.jpg)

[Dubai Marina](/wiki/Dubai_Marina "Dubai Marina")

One of the world's fastest-growing economies,[[132]](#cite_note-133) Dubai's gross domestic product is projected at over US$177 billion in 2021, with a growth rate of 6.1% in 2014.[[133]](#cite_note-134) Although a number of core elements of Dubai's trading infrastructure were built on the back of the oil industry,[[134]](#cite_note-oilgas2-135) revenues from oil and natural gas account for less than 5% of the emirate's revenues.[[135]](#cite_note-oilrev-136) It is estimated that Dubai produces 50,000 to 70,000 barrels (7,900 to 11,100 m3) of oil a day[[136]](#cite_note-137) and substantial quantities of gas from offshore fields. The emirate's share in the UAE's total gas revenues is about 2%. Dubai's oil reserves have diminished significantly and are expected to be exhausted in 20 years.[[137]](#cite_note-oilgas-138) Real estate and construction (22.6%),[[138]](#cite_note-Ddooo-139) trade (16%), *[entrepôt](/wiki/Entrep%C3%B4t "Entrepôt")* (15%), and financial services (11%) are the largest contributors to Dubai's economy.[[139]](#cite_note-dubchamber-140)

Dubai's non-oil foreign trade stood at $362 billion in 2014. Of the total trade volume, imports accounted for the largest share at $230 billion, while exports and re-exports to the emirate totaled $31 billion and $101 billion, respectively.[[140]](#cite_note-:3-141)

By 2014, China had emerged as Dubai's largest international trading partner, with total trade of $47.7 billion, up 29% from 2013. India was second among Dubai's key trading partners, with trade totaling $29.7 billion, followed by the United States at $22.62 billion. The Kingdom of Saudi Arabia was Dubai's fourth-largest trading partner globally and its first in the GCC and the Arab world, with a total trade value of $14.2 billion. Trade with Germany in 2014 totalled $12.3 billion; Switzerland and Japan both at $11.72 billion; and the UK at $10.9 billion.[[140]](#cite_note-:3-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Jebel_Ali_Port_2_Imresolt.jpg/250px-Jebel_Ali_Port_2_Imresolt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jebel_Ali_Port_2_Imresolt.jpg)

[Port of Jebel Ali](/wiki/Port_of_Jebel_Ali "Port of Jebel Ali")

Historically, Dubai and its twin across Dubai Creek, Deira (then independent of Dubai City), were important ports of call for Western manufacturers. Most of the new city's banking and financial centres were headquartered in the port area. Dubai remained a major trade hub throughout the 1970s and 1980s. Dubai has a free trade in gold and, until the 1990s, was the hub of a "brisk smuggling trade"[[64]](#cite_note-britannica-65) of gold [ingots](/wiki/Ingot "Ingot") to India, where gold import was restricted. Dubai's [Jebel Ali](/wiki/Jebel_Ali "Jebel Ali") port, constructed in the 1970s, has the largest human-made harbour in the world and was ranked seventh globally for the volume of container traffic it supports.[[141]](#cite_note-jebelali-142) Dubai is also a hub for service industries such as information technology and finance, with industry-specific [free zones](/wiki/Free_economic_zone "Free economic zone") throughout the city.[[142]](#cite_note-Business-Dubai.com-143) [Dubai Internet City](/wiki/Dubai_Internet_City "Dubai Internet City"), combined with [Dubai Media City](/wiki/Dubai_Media_City "Dubai Media City") as part of TECOM (Dubai Technology, Electronic Commerce and Media Free Zone Authority), is one such enclave, whose members include IT firms such as [Hewlett Packard Enterprise](/wiki/Hewlett_Packard_Enterprise "Hewlett Packard Enterprise"), [HP Inc.](/wiki/HP_Inc. "HP Inc."), [Halliburton](/wiki/Halliburton "Halliburton"), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), [EMC Corporation](/wiki/EMC_Corporation "EMC Corporation"), [Oracle Corporation](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation"), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), [Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell"), and [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), and media organisations such as [MBC](/wiki/Middle_East_Broadcasting_Center "Middle East Broadcasting Center"), [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN"), [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters"), [Sky News](/wiki/Sky_News "Sky News"), and AP.[[143]](#cite_note-144) Various programmes, resources, and value-added services support the growth of startups in Dubai and help them connect to new business opportunities.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/eb/National_Bank_of_Dubai_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-National_Bank_of_Dubai_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Bank_of_Dubai_-_panoramio.jpg)

[National Bank of Dubai](/wiki/National_Bank_of_Dubai "National Bank of Dubai")

The [Dubai Financial Market](/wiki/Dubai_Financial_Market "Dubai Financial Market") (DFM) was established in 2000 as a secondary market for trading securities and bonds, both local and foreign. As of the [fourth quarter](/wiki/Fourth_quarter_of_a_calendar_year "Fourth quarter of a calendar year") of 2006, its trading volume stood at about 400 billion shares, valued at $95 billion. The DFM had a [market capitalisation](/wiki/Market_capitalisation "Market capitalisation") of about $87 billion.[[114]](#cite_note-pop4-115) The other Dubai-based stock exchange is [NASDAQ Dubai](/wiki/NASDAQ_Dubai "NASDAQ Dubai"), the Middle East's international stock exchange. It enables a range of companies, including UAE and regional small and medium-sized enterprises, to trade on an exchange with an international brand name, with access to both regional and international investors.[[145]](#cite_note-146)

[DMCC](/wiki/Dubai_Multi_Commodities_Centre "Dubai Multi Commodities Centre") (Dubai Multi Commodities Centre) was established in 2002. It is the world's fastest-growing free zone and was nominated for "Global Free Zone of the Year 2016" by The *Financial Times* Magazine.

Dubai is also known as the City of Gold because a major part of the economy is based on gold trading, with Dubai's total gold trading volumes in H1 2011 reaching 580 tonnes at an average price of US$1,455 per troy ounce.[[146]](#cite_note-147)

A *City Mayors* survey ranked Dubai 44th among the world's best financial cities in 2007,[[147]](#cite_note-148) while another report by *City Mayors* indicated that Dubai was the world's 27th richest city in 2012, in terms of [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") (PPP).[[148]](#cite_note-149) Dubai is also an international [financial centre](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") (IFC) and has been ranked 37th within the top 50 global financial cities as surveyed by the MasterCard Worldwide Centres of Commerce Index (2007),[[149]](#cite_note-150) and first within the Middle East. Since it opened in September 2004, the Dubai IFC has attracted leading international firms as a regional hub and has established NASDAQ Dubai, which lists equity, derivatives, structured products, Islamic bonds ([sukuk](/wiki/Sukuk "Sukuk")), and other bonds. The Dubai IFC model is an independent risk-based regulator with a legislative system consistent with English common law.[[150]](#cite_note-151)

In 2012, the Global City Competitiveness Index by the [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit") ranked Dubai at No. 40 with a total score of 55.9. According to its 2013 research report on the future competitiveness of cities, Dubai will rank 23rd overall in the Index in 2025.[[151]](#cite_note-152) Indians, followed by Britons and Pakistanis are the top foreign investors in Dubai real estate.[[152]](#cite_note-153)

Dubai has launched several major projects to support its economy and develop different sectors. These include Dubai Fashion 2020[[153]](#cite_note-154) and [Dubai Design District](/wiki/Dubai_Design_District "Dubai Design District"),[[154]](#cite_note-155) which is expected to become a home to leading local and international designers. The AED 4 billion first phase of the project was completed in 2015.[[155]](#cite_note-156)

As of March 2024, Dubai began operating the world's biggest waste-to-energy facility, which will power approximately 135,000 homes.[[156]](#cite_note-157)

In July 2024, Dubai signed an agreement to develop a logistics hub for food, fruits, and vegetables, planned to be the world's largest of its kind. UAE Minister of Finance [Sheikh Maktoum bin Mohammed](/wiki/Maktoum_bin_Mohammed_Al_Maktoum "Maktoum bin Mohammed Al Maktoum") was present at the signing.[[157]](#cite_note-158)

In 2024, the city of Dubai ranked 12 out of 142 cities in the Smart City Index.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

### Real estate and property

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Dubai_Creek_from_Bur_Dubai_%285374118618%29.jpg/250px-Dubai_Creek_from_Bur_Dubai_%285374118618%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Creek_from_Bur_Dubai_(5374118618).jpg)

Dubai Creek, which separates [Deira](/wiki/Deira,_Dubai "Deira, Dubai") from [Bur Dubai](/wiki/Bur_Dubai "Bur Dubai"), played a vital role in the economic development of the city.

In September 2019, Dubai's ruler Sheikh [Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum "Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum") ordered the establishment of the Higher Committee for Real Estate Planning to study and evaluate future real estate construction projects, in order to achieve a balance between supply and demand,[[159]](#cite_note-160) which is seen as a move to curb the pace of construction projects following a decline in property prices.[[160]](#cite_note-161)

The government's decision to diversify from a trade-based, oil-reliant economy to one oriented toward services and tourism increased [property](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate") value, driving property appreciation from 2004 to 2006. A longer-term assessment of Dubai's property market, however, showed depreciation; some properties lost as much as 64% of their value from 2001 to November 2008.[[161]](#cite_note-162) The large-scale real estate development projects have led to the construction of some of the tallest skyscrapers and largest projects in the world such as the [Emirates Towers](/wiki/Emirates_Towers "Emirates Towers"), the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa"), the [Palm Islands](/wiki/Palm_Islands "Palm Islands"), and the most expensive hotel, the [Burj Al Arab](/wiki/Burj_Al_Arab "Burj Al Arab").[[162]](#cite_note-burjalarab-163) Dubai's property market experienced a major downturn in 2008[[163]](#cite_note-164) and 2009 as a result of the slowing economic climate.[[164]](#cite_note-Propertywire.com-165) By early 2009, the situation had worsened with the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") taking a heavy toll on property values, construction, and employment.[[165]](#cite_note-166) This has had a major impact on property investors in the region, some of whom were unable to release funds from investments made in property developments.[[166]](#cite_note-167) As of February 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dubai&action=edit), Dubai's foreign debt was estimated at $80 billion. However, this is a tiny fraction of the world's sovereign debt.[[167]](#cite_note-168)

In Dubai, many of the property owners are residents or genuine investors. However, 2020 Data from the [Centre for Advanced Defense Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Advanced_Defense_Studies "Center for Advanced Defense Studies") (C4ADS) revealed that several real estate owners in the city were either subject to international sanctions or involved in criminal activities. Some others were public officials, with little likelihood of purchasing it with their known incomes. The report "Dubai Uncovered" names 100 [Russian oligarchs](/wiki/Russian_oligarchs "Russian oligarchs"), public officials, and Europeans involved in [money laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering"). Benefiting from Dubai's lack of robust real estate regulations, several corrupt individuals owned properties abroad, laundered illicit funds, and invested in real estate to store their wealth. Names of some of such questionable figures included [Daniel Kinahan](/wiki/Daniel_Kinahan "Daniel Kinahan"), [Alexander Borodai](/wiki/Alexander_Borodai "Alexander Borodai"), Roman Lyabikhov, Tibor Bokor, [Ruslan Baisarov](/wiki/Ruslan_Baisarov "Ruslan Baisarov"), Miroslav Výboh, and others.[[168]](#cite_note-169)

For years, Dubai has been labeled a major hub for illicit cash laundering, primarily through its real estate market. Due to the UAE's lack of robust regulations and extradition treaties with many countries, fugitives have found it a perfect hideout. The "Dubai Unlocked" investigation by journalists from 75 media outlets, in coordination with OCCRP and E24, revealed how Dubai's real estate market became a haven for criminals, money launderers, drug lords, fugitives, political figures accused of corruption, and sanctioned individuals seeking to hide their money there. The investigation was based on data leaks from 2020 and 2022, primarily from the Dubai Land Department and publicly owned utility companies. The EU Tax Observatory and Norway's Centre for Tax Research estimated that, in 2022, foreign ownership of real estate in Dubai was approximately $160 billion. Until 2022, there was no obligation for real estate agents, brokers, and lawyers in Dubai to report large cash or cryptocurrency transactions to authorities.[[169]](#cite_note-170)

Following the February 2022 invasion of Ukraine, property prices in Dubai rose sharply as wealthy Russians began investing in the UAE's real estate market. Since 2020, property prices in Dubai have increased by 124%. Meanwhile, the EU Tax Observatory and Norway's Centre for Tax Research reported that Russians have invested around $6.3 billion in Dubai's existing and under-development properties since 2022. The increase in property prices pushed out the British expatriates, and forced them to look for property outside Dubai in cities like Ras Al Khaimah.[[170]](#cite_note-171)

Despite rising property prices, real estate transactions in Dubai reached an all-time high in 2024, totaling 180,987 and worth AED 522.5bn. This marks a 36.5% increase in transaction volume since 2023.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

### Tourism and retail

See also: [Tourism in Dubai](/wiki/Tourism_in_Dubai "Tourism in Dubai") and [List of tourist attractions in Dubai](/wiki/List_of_tourist_attractions_in_Dubai "List of tourist attractions in Dubai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/f3/Hotel_Atlantis_at_Sunset%2C_The_Palm_-_Dubai_%2849510861268%29.jpg/250px-Hotel_Atlantis_at_Sunset%2C_The_Palm_-_Dubai_%2849510861268%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hotel_Atlantis_at_Sunset,_The_Palm_-_Dubai_(49510861268).jpg)

[Hotel Atlantis in Dubai](/wiki/Atlantis_The_Palm,_Dubai "Atlantis The Palm, Dubai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Ain_Dubai%2C_light_show_of_the_ferris_wheel_located_in_Dubai%2C_United_Arab_Emirates.jpg/250px-Ain_Dubai%2C_light_show_of_the_ferris_wheel_located_in_Dubai%2C_United_Arab_Emirates.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ain_Dubai,_light_show_of_the_ferris_wheel_located_in_Dubai,_United_Arab_Emirates.jpg)

[Ain Dubai](/wiki/Ain_Dubai "Ain Dubai")

Tourism is an essential part of the Dubai government's strategy to maintain the flow of foreign cash. Dubai's lure for tourists is based mainly on shopping,[[172]](#cite_note-173)[[173]](#cite_note-174) but also on its ancient and modern attractions.[[174]](#cite_note-175) As of 2018, Dubai is the fourth-most-visited city in the world based on the number of international visitors and the fastest growing, increasing at a 10.7% rate.[[175]](#cite_note-176) The city hosted 14.9 million overnight visitors in 2016 and was expected to reach 20 million tourists by 2020.[[176]](#cite_note-177) A great tourist attraction in Dubai is the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa"), the tallest building in the world, although [Jeddah Tower](/wiki/Jeddah_Tower "Jeddah Tower") in [Jeddah](/wiki/Jeddah "Jeddah"), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") is aiming to be taller.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/DubaiMallAquariumDSC_7260.JPG/250px-DubaiMallAquariumDSC_7260.JPG)](/wiki/File:DubaiMallAquariumDSC_7260.JPG)

[The Dubai Mall](/wiki/The_Dubai_Mall "The Dubai Mall") Aquarium

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Dubai_fountain_during_a_show_%28Pixabay%29.jpg/250px-Dubai_fountain_during_a_show_%28Pixabay%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_fountain_during_a_show_(Pixabay).jpg)

[The Dubai Fountain](/wiki/The_Dubai_Fountain "The Dubai Fountain") in Burj Khalifa lake, Downtown Dubai

Dubai has been called the "shopping capital of the Middle East".[[177]](#cite_note-178) Dubai alone has more than 70 shopping centres, including the world's second-largest shopping centre, [The Dubai Mall](/wiki/The_Dubai_Mall "The Dubai Mall"). As of June 2024, the Emirate real estate developer [Emaar Properties](/wiki/Emaar_Properties "Emaar Properties") announced plans to expand the 12-million-square-foot mall for 1.5 billion dirhams ($408 million), adding 240 luxury stores, along with new food outlets.[[178]](#cite_note-179) Dubai is also known for the historical [souk](/wiki/Souk "Souk") districts on either side of its creek. Traditionally, [dhows](/wiki/Dhow "Dhow") from East Asia, China, Sri Lanka, and India discharged their cargo, and the goods would be bargained over in the souks adjacent to the docks. Dubai Creek played a vital role in sustaining the city's community life and was the resource that originally drove Dubai's economic boom.[[179]](#cite_note-180) As of September 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dubai&action=edit), Dubai Creek has been proposed as a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site").[[180]](#cite_note-181) Many boutiques and jeweler stores are in the city. Dubai is also known as "the City of Gold", as the [Gold Souk](/wiki/Dubai_Gold_Souk "Dubai Gold Souk") in Deira houses nearly 250 gold retail shops.[[181]](#cite_note-182)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Burj_Al-Arab_%2813996844503%29.jpg/250px-Burj_Al-Arab_%2813996844503%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burj_Al-Arab_(13996844503).jpg)

View of the Palm Jumeirah and [Burj Al Arab](/wiki/Burj_Al_Arab "Burj Al Arab")

Dubai Creek Park in Dubai Creek also plays a vital role in Dubai tourism as it showcases some of the most famous [tourist attractions in Dubai](/wiki/Tourist_attractions_in_Dubai "Tourist attractions in Dubai") such as the Dolphinarium, Cable Car, Camel Ride, Horse Carriage, and Exotic Birds Shows.[[182]](#cite_note-183)

Dubai has a wide range of parks like Safa Park, Mushrif Park, and Hamriya Park. Each is uniquely distinctive. Mushrif Park showcases different houses from around the world. A visitor can view the architectural features of both the exterior and interior of each home.

Some of the most popular beaches in Dubai are Umm Suqeim Beach, Al Mamzar Beach Park, JBR Open Beach, Kite Beach, Black Palace Beach, and Royal Island Beach Club. [Mastercard](/wiki/Mastercard "Mastercard")'s Global Destination Cities Index 2019 found that tourists spend more in Dubai than in any other city. In 2018, the country topped the list for the fourth consecutive year, with total spend of $30.82 billion. The average daily spend was $553.[[183]](#cite_note-184)

In 2019, Dubai loosened its liquor laws, allowing tourists to purchase alcohol from state-controlled stores. Previously, alcohol was accessible only to locals with special licences. The policy shift came as the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") witnessed a severe economic crisis that led to a drop in alcohol sales by volume.[[184]](#cite_note-185)

In 2021, the UAE was ranked among the 20 most dangerous places for LGBTQ tourists to visit.[[185]](#cite_note-186) In 2022, several LGBTQ tourists who travelled to Dubai were deported. In March 2022, Thai transgender model Rachaya Noppakaroon visited Dubai to perform at the Expo 2020, but was sent back because her passport gave her sex as male.[[186]](#cite_note-187) In another case, a French influencer on [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok") and [Snapchat](/wiki/Snapchat "Snapchat"), Ibrahim Godin, was sent back from Dubai because the authorities assumed his male friend travelling with him was his boyfriend. Ibrahim filed a complaint for "public defamation because of sexual orientation", and an investigation was opened by the [Vesoul](/wiki/Vesoul "Vesoul") police. He said, "Dubai is not all pretty, all rosy as we see on social networks."[[187]](#cite_note-188)[[188]](#cite_note-189)

### Expo 2020

See also: [Expo 2020](/wiki/Expo_2020 "Expo 2020")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Dubai_Expo_Sign.jpg/250px-Dubai_Expo_Sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Expo_Sign.jpg)

Sign of Expo 2020 Dubai UAE at [Dubai International Airport](/wiki/Dubai_International_Airport "Dubai International Airport")

On 2 November 2011, four cities had their bids for Expo 2020[[189]](#cite_note-:6-190) already lodged, with Dubai making a last-minute entry. The delegation from the Bureau International des Expositions, which visited Dubai in February 2013 to examine its readiness for the largest exposition, was impressed by the infrastructure and level of national support. In May 2013, the Dubai Expo 2020 Master Plan was revealed.[[190]](#cite_note-191) Dubai won the right to host Expo 2020 on 27 November 2013.[[191]](#cite_note-192)

The leading site of Dubai Expo 2020 was planned to be a 438-hectare area (1,083 acres), part of the new *Dubai Trade Centre Jebel Ali* urban development, located midway between Dubai and Abu Dhabi.[[192]](#cite_note-193) Moreover, the Expo 2020 also created various social enlistment projects and monetary boons to the city targeting the year 2020, such as initiating the world's largest solar power project.[[193]](#cite_note-194)

The Dubai Expo 2020 was scheduled to take place from 20 October 2020 until 10 April 2021 for 173 days where there would be 192 country pavilions featuring narratives from every part of the globe, have different thematic districts that would promote learning the wildlife in the forest exhibit too many other experiences.[[194]](#cite_note-195)

Due to the impact of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19"), the organisers of Expo 2020 postponed the Expo by one year to begin in 2021 (the new dates are 1 October 2021 to 31 March 2022).[[195]](#cite_note-196)[[196]](#cite_note-197)

Dubai has targets to build an inclusive, barrier-free, and disability-friendly city, which opened as [Expo City Dubai](/wiki/Expo_City_Dubai "Expo City Dubai"). The city has already implemented changes, including wheelchair-accessible taxis, sloped pavements, and tactile floor indicators at all metro stations for visually impaired passengers.[[197]](#cite_note-198)

## Architecture

See also: [List of tallest buildings in Dubai](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Dubai "List of tallest buildings in Dubai") and [Developments in Dubai](/wiki/Developments_in_Dubai "Developments in Dubai")

Photographic [silhouette](/wiki/Silhouette "Silhouette") of the [Downtown Dubai](/wiki/Downtown_Dubai "Downtown Dubai")'s [skyline](/wiki/Skyline "Skyline"); [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa"), the [world's tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings "List of tallest buildings"), is visible at the center
