[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/68/Museum_of_the_Future_12.jpg/250px-Museum_of_the_Future_12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_of_the_Future_12.jpg)

[Museum of the Future](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Future "Museum of the Future")

Dubai has a rich collection of buildings and structures of various [architectural styles](/wiki/Architectural_styles "Architectural styles"). Many modern interpretations of [Islamic architecture](/wiki/Islamic_architecture "Islamic architecture") are found here, due to a boom in construction and architectural innovation in the Arab World in general, and in Dubai in particular, supported not only by top Arab or international architectural and engineering design firms such as [Al Hashemi](/wiki/Al_Hashemi "Al Hashemi") and [Aedas](/wiki/Aedas "Aedas"), but also by top firms of New York and Chicago.[[33]](#cite_note-hist_karim-34) As a result of this boom, modern Islamic – and world – architecture has literally been taken to new levels in skyscraper building design and technology. Dubai now has more completed or topped-out skyscrapers higher than 2⁄3 km (2,200 ft), 1⁄3 km (1,100 ft), or 1⁄4 km (820 ft) [than any other city](/wiki/List_of_cities_with_most_skyscrapers "List of cities with most skyscrapers"). A culmination point was reached in 2010 with the completion of the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa") (Khalifa Tower), now by far the world's tallest building at 829.8 m (2,722 ft). The Burj Khalifa's design is derived from the patterning systems embodied in Islamic architecture, with the triple-lobed footprint of the building based on an abstracted version of the desert flower [hymenocallis](/wiki/Hymenocallis "Hymenocallis") which is native to the Dubai region.[[198]](#cite_note-199)

The completion of the Burj Khalifa, following the construction boom that began in the 1980s, accelerated in the 1990s and reached a rapid pace during the 2000s, leaving Dubai with the world's tallest skyline as of 4 January 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dubai&action=edit).[[199]](#cite_note-200)[[200]](#cite_note-201) [At The Top](/wiki/At_the_Top_(Burj_Khalifa) "At the Top (Burj Khalifa)") in Burj Khalifa, the world's second highest observatory deck after the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower "Shanghai Tower") with an outdoor terrace, is one of Dubai's most popular tourist attractions, with over 1.87 million visitors in 2013.[[201]](#cite_note-202)

The [Creek Tower](/wiki/Dubai_Creek_Tower "Dubai Creek Tower") had been planned in the 2010s to keep Dubai atop the list of [tallest buildings](/wiki/List_of_tallest_towers "List of tallest towers").[[202]](#cite_note-203) However, construction was placed on indefinite hold during the [coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). No date has been announced for the project's continuation.[[203]](#cite_note-204) Dubai is recognised as a "[Design City](/wiki/Design_Cities_(UNESCO) "Design Cities (UNESCO)")" by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s [Creative Cities Network](/wiki/Creative_Cities_Network "Creative Cities Network").

### Burj Al Arab

Main article: [Burj Al Arab](/wiki/Burj_Al_Arab "Burj Al Arab")

The Burj Al Arab (Arabic: برج العرب, *Tower of the Arabs*), a [luxury](/wiki/Luxury_resort "Luxury resort") [hotel](/wiki/Hotel "Hotel"), is frequently called "the world's only [7-star](/wiki/Hotel_rating "Hotel rating")", though its management has never made that claim. The term "7-star hotel" was coined by a British journalist to describe their experience of the hotel.[[204]](#cite_note-NationalBAA-205) A Jumeirah Group spokesperson said: "There's not a lot we can do to stop it. We're not encouraging the use of the term. We've never used it in our advertising."[[204]](#cite_note-NationalBAA-205) The hotel opened in December 1999.

### Burj Khalifa

Main article: [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Dubai_Police_Agusta_A-109K-2_in_flight_at_sunset.jpg/250px-Dubai_Police_Agusta_A-109K-2_in_flight_at_sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Police_Agusta_A-109K-2_in_flight_at_sunset.jpg)

Dubai Police Agusta A-109K-2 in flight near Burj Khalifa

Burj Khalifa, known as the Burj Dubai before its inauguration, is a 828 metres (2,717 ft) high[[205]](#cite_note-206) skyscraper in Dubai, and the tallest building in the world. The structure of the desert flower inspired the tower *[Hymenocallis](/wiki/Hymenocallis "Hymenocallis")*. It was constructed by more than 30 contracting companies worldwide, with workers from 100 nationalities, from 2004 to 2009. It's also an architectural icon, named after Sheikh [Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan](/wiki/Khalifa_bin_Zayed_Al_Nahyan "Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan").[[206]](#cite_note-207) The building opened on 4 January 2010.[[207]](#cite_note-208)

### Palm Jumeirah

Main article: [Palm Jumeirah](/wiki/Palm_Jumeirah "Palm Jumeirah")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Dubai_Wingsuit_Flying_Trip_%287623566780%29.jpg/250px-Dubai_Wingsuit_Flying_Trip_%287623566780%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Wingsuit_Flying_Trip_(7623566780).jpg)

The [Palm Jumeirah](/wiki/Palm_Jumeirah "Palm Jumeirah")

The Palm Jumeirah is an [artificial](/wiki/Artificial_island "Artificial island") [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago"), created using [land reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") by [Nakheel Properties](/wiki/Nakheel_Properties "Nakheel Properties"), a company owned by the Dubai government, and designed and developed by Helman Hurley Charvat Peacock/Architects, Inc. It is one of three planned islands, the [Palm Islands](/wiki/Palm_Islands "Palm Islands"), which extend into the Persian Gulf. The Palm Jumeirah is the smallest and the original of the three Palm Islands, and is in the [Jumeirah](/wiki/Jumeirah "Jumeirah") coastal area. It was built from 2001 to 2006.[[208]](#cite_note-thepalmae-209)

### The World Islands

Main article: [The World (archipelago)](/wiki/The_World_(archipelago) "The World (archipelago)")

The World Islands is an [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") of small artificial islands shaped like a world map in the waters of the Persian Gulf, 4.0 kilometres (2.5 mi) off the coast of Dubai.[[209]](#cite_note-210) The World islands are mainly made of sand dredged from Dubai's shallow coastal waters and are one of several artificial island developments in Dubai. The islands' areas vary from 250,000 to 900,000 square feet. The islands are arranged in a world map shape, and the archipelago spans about 9 km.[[210]](#cite_note-211)

### Dubai Miracle Garden

Main article: [Dubai Miracle Garden](/wiki/Dubai_Miracle_Garden "Dubai Miracle Garden")

On 14 February 2013, the Dubai Miracle Garden, a 72,000-metre (236,000-foot) flower garden, opened in [Dubailand](/wiki/Dubailand "Dubailand"). It is the world's largest flower garden. The garden displays more than 50 million flowers with more than 70 species of flowering plants.[[211]](#cite_note-212) The garden uses treated wastewater from the city's municipality and utilises the drip irrigation method for watering the plants. During the summer seasons from late May to September, when the climate can get extremely hot with an average high of about 40 °C (104 °F), the garden stays closed.[[212]](#cite_note-213)[[213]](#cite_note-214)

### Aeternitas Tower

In January 2024, Dubai announced its intention to build the [Aeternitas](/wiki/Aeternitas "Aeternitas") Tower, which will become the world's tallest residential clock tower at a height of 450 m (1,480 ft). That is four times the height of [London](/wiki/London "London")'s [Big Ben](/wiki/Big_Ben "Big Ben"). The tallest clock tower to date is [Makkah Clock Royal Tower](/wiki/The_Clock_Towers "The Clock Towers") in Mecca. The tower's name, "Aeternitas", comes from the ancient [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Religion "Roman Religion") religion; she was the divine personification of eternity. The project is a joint venture between Dubai-based real estate developer London Gate and Swiss luxury watch manufacturer [Franck Muller](/wiki/Franck_Muller "Franck Muller").[[214]](#cite_note-215) The tower offers luxury apartments, including 1 to 4-bedroom units, as well as Sky Villas and Sky Mansions, with interiors inspired by Franck Muller's designs.[[215]](#cite_note-216)

### Dubai Marina

Main article: [Dubai Marina](/wiki/Dubai_Marina "Dubai Marina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/View_from_the_Torch_-_64th_floor.jpg/250px-View_from_the_Torch_-_64th_floor.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_from_the_Torch_-_64th_floor.jpg)

[Dubai Marina](/wiki/Dubai_Marina "Dubai Marina")

Dubai Marina is an artificial canal city, built along a 3-kilometre (2 mi) stretch of Persian Gulf shoreline. As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dubai&action=edit), it had a population of 55,052.[[216]](#cite_note-217) When the entire development is complete, it will accommodate more than 120,000 people in residential towers and villas.[[217]](#cite_note-designbuild-network.com-218) The 50 million square feet area of the Dubai Marina boasts a centrepiece that includes a 3.5-kilometer water canal which is the heart of the development and provides dual access to the sea, making Dubai Marina a foremost sailing destination.[[218]](#cite_note-219) It is on Interchange 5 between [Jebel Ali Port](/wiki/Jebel_Ali_Port "Jebel Ali Port") and the area which hosts [Dubai Internet City](/wiki/Dubai_Internet_City "Dubai Internet City"), [Dubai Media City](/wiki/Dubai_Media_City "Dubai Media City"), and the [American University in Dubai](/wiki/American_University_in_Dubai "American University in Dubai"). The first phase of this project has been completed. Dubai Marina was inspired by the [Concord Pacific Place](/wiki/Concord_Pacific_Place "Concord Pacific Place") development along [False Creek](/wiki/False_Creek "False Creek") in [Vancouver, BC](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), Canada.[[219]](#cite_note-220) Much marine wildlife (especially whales and sharks) has entered the marina because of its proximity to the open sea.[[220]](#cite_note-221)

### Address Beach Resort and Address Beach Residences

The structure consists of two towers connected at the bottom, with a [sky bridge](/wiki/Sky_bridge "Sky bridge") at the top that connects the 63rd through the 77th levels. The sky bridge houses luxury apartments on the world's highest occupiable floor at 294.36 metres.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Known as Jumeirah Gate, it opened in December 2020 and is situated along the beach. The towers have the world's highest [infinity pool](/wiki/Infinity_pool "Infinity pool") in a building, on the roof, at a height of 293.906 metres.[[221]](#cite_note-222)

## Transportation

Main article: [Transportation in Dubai](/wiki/Transportation_in_Dubai "Transportation in Dubai")

Transport in Dubai is controlled by the [Roads and Transport Authority (RTA)](/wiki/Roads_and_Transport_Authority_(Dubai) "Roads and Transport Authority (Dubai)"), an agency of the government of Dubai, formed by royal decree in 2005.[[222]](#cite_note-223) The public transport network has in the past faced congestion and reliability issues which a large investment programme has addressed, including over AED 70 billion of improvements planned for completion by 2020, when the population of the city is projected to exceed 3.5 million.[[223]](#cite_note-224) In 2009, according to Dubai Municipality statistics, there were an estimated 1,021,880 cars in Dubai.[[224]](#cite_note-225) In January 2010, the proportion of Dubai residents who use public transport stood at 6%.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

### Road

See also: [List of roads in Dubai](/wiki/List_of_roads_in_Dubai "List of roads in Dubai"), [Dubai route numbering system](/wiki/Dubai_route_numbering_system "Dubai route numbering system"), and [List of bridges and tunnels in Dubai](/wiki/List_of_bridges_and_tunnels_in_Dubai "List of bridges and tunnels in Dubai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Tolerance_Bridge.jpg/250px-Tolerance_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tolerance_Bridge.jpg)

Tolerance Bridge of [Business Bay](/wiki/Business_Bay "Business Bay")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Dubai_Road_2013.JPG/250px-Dubai_Road_2013.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Road_2013.JPG)

[E 11 Road](/wiki/E_11_road_(United_Arab_Emirates) "E 11 road (United Arab Emirates)")

Five main routes – [E 11](/wiki/E_11_(UAE) "E 11 (UAE)") (Sheikh Zayed Road), [E 311](/wiki/E_311_(UAE) "E 311 (UAE)") (Sheikh Mohammed Bin Zayed Road), [E 44](/wiki/E_44_road_(United_Arab_Emirates) "E 44 road (United Arab Emirates)") (Dubai-Hatta Highway), [E 77](/wiki/E_77_(UAE) "E 77 (UAE)") (Dubai-Al Habab Road), and [E 66](/wiki/E_66_road_(United_Arab_Emirates) "E 66 road (United Arab Emirates)") (Oud Metha Road, Dubai-[Al Ain](/wiki/Al_Ain "Al Ain") Road, or Tahnoun Bin Mohammad Al Nahyan Road)[[226]](#cite_note-GulfNews_11-2018-227) – run through Dubai, connecting the city to other towns and emirates. Additionally, several important intra-city routes, such as [D 89](/wiki/D_89_road_(United_Arab_Emirates) "D 89 road (United Arab Emirates)") (Al Maktoum Road/Airport Road), [D 85](/wiki/D_85_road_(United_Arab_Emirates) "D 85 road (United Arab Emirates)") (Baniyas Road), [D 75](/wiki/D_75_road_(United_Arab_Emirates) "D 75 road (United Arab Emirates)") (Sheikh Rashid Road), [D 73](/wiki/D_73_road_(United_Arab_Emirates) "D 73 road (United Arab Emirates)") (Al Dhiyafa Road now named as 2 December street), [D 94](/wiki/D_94_road_(United_Arab_Emirates) "D 94 road (United Arab Emirates)") (Jumeirah Road), and [D 92](/wiki/D_92_road_(United_Arab_Emirates) "D 92 road (United Arab Emirates)") (Al Khaleej/Al Wasl Road) connect the various localities in the city. The eastern and western sections of the city are connected by [Al Maktoum Bridge](/wiki/Al_Maktoum_Bridge "Al Maktoum Bridge"), [Al Garhoud Bridge](/wiki/Al_Garhoud_Bridge "Al Garhoud Bridge"), [Al Shindagha Tunnel](/wiki/Al_Shindagha_Tunnel "Al Shindagha Tunnel"), [Business Bay Crossing](/wiki/Business_Bay_Crossing "Business Bay Crossing"), and [Floating Bridge](/wiki/Floating_Bridge,_Dubai "Floating Bridge, Dubai").[[227]](#cite_note-228)

The RTA operates Dubai's public bus transport system. The bus system services 140 routes and transports over 109 million people in 2008. By the end of 2010, there will be 2,100 buses in service across the city.[[228]](#cite_note-229) In 2006, the transport authority announced the construction of 500 air-conditioned passenger bus shelters, and planned for 1,000 more across the emirates in a move to encourage the use of public buses.[[229]](#cite_note-230)

The RTA licenses all taxi services. Dubai-licensed taxis are easily identifiable by their cream bodywork, with varied roof colours indicating the operator. Dubai Taxi Corporation, a division of the RTA, is the largest operator and operates red-roofed taxis. There are five private operators: Metro Taxis (orange roofs); Network Taxis (yellow roofs); Cars Taxis (blue roofs); Arabia Taxis (green roofs); and City Taxis (purple roofs). In addition, there is a Ladies and Families taxi service (pink roofs) with female drivers, which caters exclusively to women and children. More than 3,000 taxis operate in the emirate, averaging 192,000 trips daily and carrying about 385,000 people. In 2009, taxi trips exceeded 70 million trips, serving around 140.45 million passengers.[[230]](#cite_note-231)[[231]](#cite_note-232)[[232]](#cite_note-233)

### Air

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Dubai_-_International_%28DXB_-_OMDB%29_AN1204297.jpg/250px-Dubai_-_International_%28DXB_-_OMDB%29_AN1204297.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_-_International_(DXB_-_OMDB)_AN1204297.jpg)

[Dubai International Airport](/wiki/Dubai_International_Airport "Dubai International Airport") is the busiest airport in the world by international passenger traffic.[[233]](#cite_note-234)

[Dubai International Airport](/wiki/Dubai_International_Airport "Dubai International Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): DXB), the hub for the [Emirates](/wiki/Emirates_(airline) "Emirates (airline)") airline, serves the city of Dubai and other emirates in the country. The airport is the [third-busiest airport in the world by passenger traffic](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "World's busiest airports by passenger traffic") and the [world's busiest airport by international passenger traffic](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_international_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by international passenger traffic").[[234]](#cite_note-235) In addition to being an important passenger traffic hub, the airport is the [sixth-busiest cargo airport in world](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_airports_by_cargo_traffic "World's busiest airports by cargo traffic"), handling 2.37 million tons of cargo in 2014.[[235]](#cite_note-236) Emirates is one of the national flag carriers of the United Arab Emirates.[[236]](#cite_note-237) As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dubai&action=edit), it operated internationally, serving over 150 destinations in over 70 countries across six continents.[[237]](#cite_note-238)

The development of [Al Maktoum International Airport](/wiki/Al_Maktoum_International_Airport "Al Maktoum International Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): DWC) was announced in 2004. The first phase of the airport, featuring one A380-capable runway, 64 remote stands, one cargo terminal with an annual capacity for 250,000 tonnes of cargo, and a passenger terminal building designed to accommodate five million passengers per year, has been opened.[[238]](#cite_note-239) When completed, Dubai World Central-Al Maktoum International will be the largest airport in the world with five runways, four terminal buildings, and capacity for 160 million passengers and 12 million tons of cargo.[[239]](#cite_note-240)[[240]](#cite_note-241)

### Rail

Main articles: [Dubai Metro](/wiki/Dubai_Metro "Dubai Metro"), [Palm Jumeirah Monorail](/wiki/Palm_Jumeirah_Monorail "Palm Jumeirah Monorail"), and [Dubai Tram](/wiki/Dubai_Tram "Dubai Tram")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Dubai_Blue_Line_stel_5112_to_Creek_%2852765713036%29.jpg/250px-Dubai_Blue_Line_stel_5112_to_Creek_%2852765713036%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Blue_Line_stel_5112_to_Creek_(52765713036).jpg)

[Dubai Metro](/wiki/Dubai_Metro "Dubai Metro") is the first kind of rail transportation in the UAE, and is the Arabian Peninsula's first urban train network.[[241]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-242)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Dubai_Tram_Alstom_Citadis_402_near_Al_Sufouh.jpg/250px-Dubai_Tram_Alstom_Citadis_402_near_Al_Sufouh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Tram_Alstom_Citadis_402_near_Al_Sufouh.jpg)

[Dubai Tram](/wiki/Dubai_Tram "Dubai Tram") is one of the first completely [ground-level power supply](/wiki/Ground-level_power_supply "Ground-level power supply")-based tram networks in the world.[[242]](#cite_note-243)

[Dubai Metro](/wiki/Dubai_Metro "Dubai Metro") consists of two lines (Red and Green) that run through the city's financial and residential areas. It was opened in September 2009.[[243]](#cite_note-244) UK-based international service company [Serco](/wiki/Serco "Serco") is responsible for operating the metro. The [Red Line](/wiki/Red_Line_(Dubai_Metro) "Red Line (Dubai Metro)") is the major backbone, with 29 stations (4 underground, 24 elevated, and one at ground level) running from Rashidiya Station to UAE Xchange Station in Jebel Ali. The [Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(Dubai_Metro) "Green Line (Dubai Metro)"), running from the Etisalat Station to the Creek Station, has 20 stations (8 underground, 12 elevated). An extension to the Red Line connecting the EXPO 2020 site opened on 1 June 2021. A [Blue](/wiki/Blue_Line_(Dubai_Metro) "Blue Line (Dubai Metro)") and a [Purple Line](/wiki/Purple_Line_(Dubai_Metro) "Purple Line (Dubai Metro)") have also been planned. The Dubai Metro is the first urban train network in the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula").[[241]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-242) The trains are fully automated and driverless.[[244]](#cite_note-245)

A [monorail](/wiki/Monorail "Monorail") line connecting the Palm Jumeirah to the mainland opened on 30 April 2009.[[245]](#cite_note-timeout-246) It is the first monorail in the Middle East.[[246]](#cite_note-247) An extension to connect to the [Red Line](/wiki/Red_Line_(Dubai_Metro) "Red Line (Dubai Metro)") of the [Dubai Metro](/wiki/Dubai_Metro "Dubai Metro") is planned.[[247]](#cite_note-gn-248)

A tramway located in Al Sufouh runs for 14.5 km (9.0 mi) along Al Sufouh Road from Dubai Marina to the Burj Al Arab and the Mall of the Emirates with two interchanges with the Dubai Metro Red Line. The first section, a 10.6 km (6.6 mi)- long tram line serving 11 stations, was opened in 2014.[[248]](#cite_note-249)

Dubai has announced it will complete a link of the UAE high-speed rail system, which is planned to link with the whole GCC (Gulf Cooperation Council, also known as [Cooperation Council for the Arab States of the Gulf](/wiki/Cooperation_Council_for_the_Arab_States_of_the_Gulf "Cooperation Council for the Arab States of the Gulf")), and then possibly [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The high-speed rail will support passengers and cargo.[[249]](#cite_note-250)

### Waterways

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Madinat_Jumeirah_dhow_%283050971546%29.jpg/250px-Madinat_Jumeirah_dhow_%283050971546%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madinat_Jumeirah_dhow_(3050971546).jpg)

*[Abras](/wiki/Abra_(boat) "Abra (boat)")* and *[dhows](/wiki/Dhows "Dhows")* are traditional modes of waterway transport.

There are two major commercial ports in Dubai, [Port Rashid](/wiki/Port_Rashid "Port Rashid") and [Port Jebel Ali](/wiki/Jebel_Ali "Jebel Ali"). Port Jebel Ali is the world's largest human-made harbour, the biggest port in the Middle East,[[250]](#cite_note-251) and the 7th-busiest port in the world.[[141]](#cite_note-jebelali-142) One of the more traditional methods of getting across [Bur Dubai](/wiki/Bur_Dubai "Bur Dubai") to [Deira](/wiki/Deira,_Dubai "Deira, Dubai") is by *[abras](/wiki/Abra_(boat) "Abra (boat)")*, small boats that ferry passengers across the [Dubai Creek](/wiki/Dubai_Creek "Dubai Creek"), between abra stations in Bastakiya and [Baniyas Road](/wiki/Baniyas_Road "Baniyas Road").[[251]](#cite_note-252) The Marine Transport Agency has also implemented the Dubai Water Bus System. The water bus is a fully air-conditioned boat service connecting selected destinations across the creek. One can also use the tourist water bus service in Dubai. The latest addition to the water transport system is the Water Taxi.[[252]](#cite_note-253) The Water-Taxis can transport a maximum of 20 passengers at a time which provides a smooth journey along Al Mamzar and Dubai Marina via Dubai Creek. It has 40 pickup points across Dubai.[[253]](#cite_note-254)

Dubai is increasingly leveraging its logistics and ports to participate in trade between Europe and China, as well as between Europe and Africa, in addition to oil transport. For this purpose, ports such as Jebel Ali and Mina Rashid are rapidly expanded, and investments are made in their technology. The country is historically and currently part of the Maritime [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road") that runs from the Chinese coast to the south via the southern tip of [India](/wiki/India "India") to [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa"), from there through the Red Sea via the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") to the Mediterranean, there to the Upper Adriatic region to the northern Italian hub of [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") with its rail connections to [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"), Eastern Europe, and the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea").[[254]](#cite_note-255)[[255]](#cite_note-256)[[256]](#cite_note-257)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Dubai](/wiki/Culture_of_Dubai "Culture of Dubai")

Further information: [Culture of the United Arab Emirates](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Culture of the United Arab Emirates")

[![Museum of the Future](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/2f/Museum_of_Future_-_Dubai.jpg/250px-Museum_of_Future_-_Dubai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_of_Future_-_Dubai.jpg)

[![Dubai Future Forum](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Dubai_Future_Forum_2024_-_interior_01.jpg/250px-Dubai_Future_Forum_2024_-_interior_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Future_Forum_2024_-_interior_01.jpg)

[Museum of the Future](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Future "Museum of the Future") and interior, showing the annual [Dubai Future Forum](/wiki/Dubai_Future_Forum "Dubai Future Forum") (2024)

The UAE's culture mainly reflects traditional Arab culture. The influence of Arab and Islamic cultures on architecture, music, attire, cuisine, and lifestyle is also pronounced. Five times a day, Muslims are called to prayer from the [minarets](/wiki/Minaret "Minaret") of mosques scattered across the country. Major holidays in Dubai include [Eid al-Fitr](/wiki/Eid_al-Fitr "Eid al-Fitr"), which marks the end of [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan"), and [National Day](/wiki/National_Day "National Day") (2 December), which marks the formation of the United Arab Emirates.[[257]](#cite_note-258)

The city's cultural imprint as a small, ethnically homogeneous pearling community was transformed by the arrival of other ethnic groups and nationals—first by Iranians in the early 1900s, and later by Indians and Pakistanis in the 1960s. In 2005, 84% of Dubai's metropolitan population was foreign-born, about half of whom were from India.[[112]](#cite_note-migrationinformation-113)

From 2006 to 2022, the weekend had been Friday and Saturday, as a compromise between Friday's holiness to Muslims and the Western weekend of Saturday and Sunday.[[258]](#cite_note-259) Before 2006, the weekend was Thursday-Friday. On 1 January 2022, Dubai moved to a four-and-a-half-day working week, with the weekend comprising Friday afternoon, Saturday, and Sunday.[[259]](#cite_note-260)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Meydan_Beach_Club%2C_Dubai_%288668492594%29.jpg/250px-Meydan_Beach_Club%2C_Dubai_%288668492594%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meydan_Beach_Club,_Dubai_(8668492594).jpg)

Meydan Beach Club, Jumeirah

Because of the tourism-driven entrepreneurial approach of many Dubai residents and the high standard of living, Dubai's culture has gradually evolved toward luxury, opulence, and lavishness, with a strong emphasis on leisure-related extravagance.[[260]](#cite_note-261)[[261]](#cite_note-262)[[262]](#cite_note-263) Dubai is known for its nightlife. Clubs and bars are chiefly found in hotels due to liquor laws. *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* called Dubai "the kind of city where you might run into [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan") at the Buddha Bar or stumble across [Naomi Campbell](/wiki/Naomi_Campbell "Naomi Campbell") celebrating her birthday with a multiday bash".[[263]](#cite_note-nyt-264)

Annual entertainment events such as the [Dubai Shopping Festival](/wiki/Dubai_Shopping_Festival "Dubai Shopping Festival")[[264]](#cite_note-265) (DSF) and Dubai Summer Surprises (DSS) attract over 4 million visitors from across the region and generate revenues over $2.7 billion.[[265]](#cite_note-dsf-266)[[266]](#cite_note-267) The [International Festivals and Events Association](/wiki/International_Festivals_and_Events_Association "International Festivals and Events Association") (IFEA), the world's leading events trade association, has crowned Dubai as *IFEA World Festival and Event City, 2012* in the cities category with a population of more than one million.[[267]](#cite_note-268)[[268]](#cite_note-269)

Large shopping malls in the city, such as [Deira City Centre](/wiki/Deira_City_Centre "Deira City Centre"), [Mirdiff City Centre](/wiki/Mirdiff_City_Centre "Mirdiff City Centre"), [BurJuman](/wiki/BurJuman "BurJuman"), [Mall of the Emirates](/wiki/Mall_of_the_Emirates "Mall of the Emirates"), [Dubai Mall](/wiki/Dubai_Mall "Dubai Mall") (the world's second-largest), [Dubai Marina Mall](/wiki/Dubai_Marina_Mall "Dubai Marina Mall"), [Dubai Hills Mall](/wiki/Dubai_Hills_Mall "Dubai Hills Mall"), [Dragon Mart](/wiki/Dragon_Mart_(Dubai) "Dragon Mart (Dubai)"), [Dubai Festival City Mall](/wiki/Dubai_Festival_City_Mall "Dubai Festival City Mall"), and [Ibn Battuta Mall](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta_Mall "Ibn Battuta Mall") as well as traditional [Dubai Gold Souk](/wiki/Dubai_Gold_Souk "Dubai Gold Souk"), [Al Souk Al Kabir](/wiki/Al_Souk_Al_Kabir "Al Souk Al Kabir") (known as Meena Bazaar), and other *souks* attract shoppers from the region.[[269]](#cite_note-270)

### Cuisine

See also: [Emirati cuisine](/wiki/Emirati_cuisine "Emirati cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Spice_Souq_Dubai_13.jpg/250px-Spice_Souq_Dubai_13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spice_Souq_Dubai_13.jpg)

Traditional Middle Eastern spices at the [Dubai Spice Souk](/wiki/Dubai_Spice_Souk "Dubai Spice Souk") in Deira, Old Dubai

[Arabic cuisine](/wiki/Arab_cuisine "Arab cuisine") is very popular and is available everywhere in the city, from the small *[shawarma](/wiki/Shawarma "Shawarma")* diners in [Deira](/wiki/Deira,_Dubai "Deira, Dubai") and [Al Karama](/wiki/Al_Karama,_Dubai "Al Karama, Dubai") to the restaurants in Dubai's hotels. Fast food, South Asian, and Chinese cuisines are also very popular and are widely available. The sale and consumption of pork is regulated and is legally permitted to be sold only to non-Muslims, in designated areas of supermarkets and airports.[[270]](#cite_note-pork-271) Similarly, the sale of alcoholic beverages is also regulated. A liquor permit is required to purchase alcohol, but it is available at hotel bars and restaurants.[[271]](#cite_note-Dubai_Culture-272) *[Shisha](/wiki/Hookah "Hookah")* and *[qahwa](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee")* boutiques are also popular in Dubai. [Biryani](/wiki/Biryani "Biryani") is also popular in Dubai.[[272]](#cite_note-273)

The inaugural Dubai Food Festival was held between 21 February and 15 March 2014.[[273]](#cite_note-274) According to *[Vision](/wiki/Vision_(magazine) "Vision (magazine)")* magazine, the event was aimed at enhancing and celebrating Dubai's position as the gastronomic capital of the region. The festival was designed to showcase the variety of flavours and cuisines on offer in Dubai, featuring dishes from over 200 nationalities.[[274]](#cite_note-275) The next food festival was held between 23 February 2017 and 11 March 2017.[[275]](#cite_note-276)

### Entertainment

See also: [Music of the United Arab Emirates](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Music of the United Arab Emirates")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/63/DubaiOpera.jpg/250px-DubaiOpera.jpg)](/wiki/File:DubaiOpera.jpg)

[Dubai Opera](/wiki/Dubai_Opera "Dubai Opera")

Dubai Opera opened its doors on 31 August 2016 in Downtown Dubai with a performance by [Plácido Domingo](/wiki/Pl%C3%A1cido_Domingo "Plácido Domingo"). The venue is a 2000-seat, multifunctional performing arts centre that can host not only theatrical shows, concerts, and operas, but also weddings, gala dinners, banquets, and conferences. Arabic movies are popular in Dubai and the UAE. Since 2004, the city has hosted the annual [Dubai International Film Festival](/wiki/Dubai_International_Film_Festival "Dubai International Film Festival") which serves as a showcase for Arab and Middle Eastern filmmaking talent.[[276]](#cite_note-277) The [Dubai Desert Rock Festival](/wiki/Dubai_Desert_Rock_Festival "Dubai Desert Rock Festival") was also another major festival consisting of heavy metal and rock artists but is no longer held in Dubai.

One of the lesser-known sides of Dubai is the importance of its young [contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art") gallery scene. Since 2008, the leading contemporary art galleries such as Carbon 12 Dubai,[[277]](#cite_note-278) Green Art, gallery Isabelle van den Eynde, and The Third Line have brought the city onto the international art map. [Art Dubai](/wiki/Art_Dubai "Art Dubai"), the region's growing and reputable art fair, is also a major contributor to the development of the contemporary art scene.[[278]](#cite_note-279) The [Theatre of Digital Art](/wiki/Theatre_of_Digital_Art "Theatre of Digital Art") Dubai (ToDA) opened in 2020 and presents immersive [digital art](/wiki/Digital_art "Digital art"), including contemporary work.[[279]](#cite_note-280)

### Media

Main article: [Dubai Media Incorporated](/wiki/Dubai_Media_Incorporated "Dubai Media Incorporated")

See also: [List of media outlets in Dubai Media City](/wiki/List_of_media_outlets_in_Dubai_Media_City "List of media outlets in Dubai Media City"), [Radio and television channels of Dubai](/wiki/Radio_and_television_channels_of_Dubai "Radio and television channels of Dubai"), and [Studio 52](/wiki/Studio_52 "Studio 52")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Etisalat_tower_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Etisalat_tower_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Etisalat_tower_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Etisalat](/wiki/Etisalat "Etisalat")'s headquarters in Dubai

Many international news agencies, such as [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters"), [APTN](/wiki/Associated_Press_Television_News "Associated Press Television News"), [Bloomberg L.P.](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P."), and [Middle East Broadcasting Centre](/wiki/Middle_East_Broadcasting_Center "Middle East Broadcasting Center") (MBC), operate in Dubai Media City and Dubai Internet City. Additionally, several local network television channels, such as [Dubai One](/wiki/Dubai_One "Dubai One") (formerly Channel 33) and [Dubai TV](/wiki/Dubai_TV "Dubai TV") (EDTV), provide programming in English and Arabic, respectively. Dubai is also the headquarters for several print media outlets. *[Dar Al Khaleej](/wiki/Dar_Al_Khaleej "Dar Al Khaleej")*, *[Al Bayan](/wiki/Al_Bayan_(newspaper) "Al Bayan (newspaper)")*, and *[Al Ittihad](/wiki/Al-Ittihad_(Emirati_newspaper) "Al-Ittihad (Emirati newspaper)")* are the city's largest circulating Arabic language newspapers,[[280]](#cite_note-alkhaleej-281) while *[Gulf News](/wiki/Gulf_News "Gulf News")*, *[Khaleej Times](/wiki/Khaleej_Times "Khaleej Times")*, *Khaleej Mag*, and *[7days](/wiki/7days "7days")* are the largest circulating English newspapers.[[281]](#cite_note-gulfnewskhaleejtimes-282)

[Etisalat](/wiki/Etisalat "Etisalat"), the government-owned telecommunications provider, held a virtual monopoly over telecommunications services in Dubai before the establishment of other, smaller telecommunications companies, such as Emirates Integrated Telecommunications Company (EITC—better known as Du) in 2006. The Internet was introduced into the UAE (and therefore Dubai) in 1995. The network has an Internet bandwidth of 7.5 Gbit/s and supports 49 STM-1 links.[[282]](#cite_note-283) Dubai houses two of four Domain Name System (DNS) data centres in the country (DXBNIC1, DXBNIC2).[[283]](#cite_note-dxbittopology-284) Censorship is common in Dubai and used by the government to control content that it believes violates the cultural and political sensitivities of Emirates.[[284]](#cite_note-etisalatmon-285) Homosexuality, drugs, and the theory of evolution are generally considered taboo.[[271]](#cite_note-Dubai_Culture-272)[[285]](#cite_note-Bedell-286)

Internet content is regulated in Dubai. Etisalat uses a proxy server to filter Internet content the government deems inconsistent with the values of the country, such as sites that provide information on how to bypass the proxy; sites about dating, gay and lesbian networks, and pornography; and formerly sites originating from [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel").[[286]](#cite_note-287) Emirates Media and Internet (a division of Etisalat) notes that as of 2002[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dubai&action=edit), 76% of Internet users are male. About 60% of Internet users were Asian, and 25% were Arab. Dubai enacted the Electronic Transactions and Commerce Law in 2002, which addresses digital signatures and electronic registers. It prohibits Internet Service Providers (ISPs) from disclosing information gathered in the course of providing services.[[287]](#cite_note-288) The penal code contains official provisions that prohibit digital access to pornography; however, it does not address cyber crime or data protection.[[288]](#cite_note-privacy-289)

In 2019, the Italian artist [Princess Bee](/wiki/Princess_Bee "Princess Bee") produced "Hi Dubai", the first cross-media project that reveals the "soul of the city" through the life and work experiences of 25 Emirati and expat women. The series also promoted the Emirate's lifestyle and main events to attract young people to visit and live in Dubai. "Hi Dubai" was on air on the national channel Dubai One TV in prime time, after the National News, during the UAE National Day week; then distributed online on Dubai Post and in-flight on [ICE](/wiki/Emirates_(airline)#In-flight_entertainment_system_(ICE) "Emirates (airline)") across all Emirates Airlines flights.[[289]](#cite_note-290)

### Sports

Main article: [List of sports venues in Dubai](/wiki/List_of_sports_venues_in_Dubai "List of sports venues in Dubai")

See also: [Traditional sports in the United Arab Emirates](/wiki/Traditional_sports_in_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Traditional sports in the United Arab Emirates")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/DTC1.jpg/250px-DTC1.jpg)](/wiki/File:DTC1.jpg)

[Dubai Tennis Stadium](/wiki/Aviation_Club_Tennis_Centre "Aviation Club Tennis Centre")

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") are the most popular sports in Dubai. The [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") is headquartered there. Three football teams ([Al Wasl FC](/wiki/Al_Wasl_FC "Al Wasl FC"), [Shabab Al-Ahli Dubai FC](/wiki/Shabab_Al-Ahli_Dubai_FC "Shabab Al-Ahli Dubai FC"), and [Al Nasr SC](/wiki/Al_Nasr_SC_(Dubai) "Al Nasr SC (Dubai)")) represent Dubai in [UAE Pro-League](/wiki/UAE_Pro-League "UAE Pro-League").[[271]](#cite_note-Dubai_Culture-272) Al-Wasl have the second-most championships in the UAE League, after [Al Ain](/wiki/Al_Ain_FC "Al Ain FC"). Dubai also hosts both the annual [Dubai Tennis Championships](/wiki/Dubai_Tennis_Championships "Dubai Tennis Championships") and [The Legends Rock Dubai](/wiki/The_Legends_Rock_Dubai "The Legends Rock Dubai") tennis tournaments, as well as the [Dubai Desert Classic](/wiki/Dubai_Desert_Classic "Dubai Desert Classic") golf tournament and the [DP World Tour Championship](/wiki/DP_World_Tour_Championship,_Dubai "DP World Tour Championship, Dubai"), all of which attract sports stars from around the world. The [Dubai World Cup](/wiki/Dubai_World_Cup "Dubai World Cup"), a [thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") horse race, is held annually at the [Meydan Racecourse](/wiki/Meydan_Racecourse "Meydan Racecourse"). The city's top basketball team has traditionally been [Shabab Al Ahli Basket](/wiki/Shabab_Al_Ahli_Basket "Shabab Al Ahli Basket"). Dubai also hosts the traditional [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") tournament, [Dubai Sevens](/wiki/Dubai_Sevens "Dubai Sevens"), part of the [Sevens World Series](/wiki/Sevens_World_Series "Sevens World Series"). In 2009, Dubai hosted the [2009 Rugby World Cup Sevens](/wiki/2009_Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "2009 Rugby World Cup Sevens").

Auto racing is also a major sport in Dubai; the [Dubai Autodrome](/wiki/Dubai_Autodrome "Dubai Autodrome") hosts many auto racing events throughout the year. Another sporting event in Dubai is the [Dubai Run](/w/index.php?title=Dubai_Run&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dubai Run (page does not exist)"), which is part of the Dubai Fitness Challenge and the world's largest free fun run, a main highlight of the challenge since 2017.[[290]](#cite_note-291) It also features a state-of-the-art indoor and outdoor [Kartdrome](https://www.dubaiautodrome.com/kartdrome-main/racing/), popular among racing enthusiasts and recreational riders.

The Indian Premier League cricket competition was held in the UAE in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic. On 12 June, [Tommy Fleetwood](/wiki/Tommy_Fleetwood "Tommy Fleetwood"), 7-time DP World Tour winner, was declared World Global Ambassador.[[291]](#cite_note-292) In 2025, Dubai introduced the "Dubai Sports Strategy 2033", a long-term framework designed to support the emirate's sports sector and strengthen its position as a global sports destination. The strategy outlines initiatives to expand community participation in physical activity, enhance the quality and number of sporting events, and develop local talent across multiple disciplines. It also aims to increase the economic contribution of Dubai's sports industry through targeted programmes and infrastructure development.[[292]](#cite_note-293)

### Dress code

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Al_Tayer_Motors_Sponsors_High-class_Dubai_World_Cup_Carnival_%288490930005%29.jpg/250px-Al_Tayer_Motors_Sponsors_High-class_Dubai_World_Cup_Carnival_%288490930005%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Al_Tayer_Motors_Sponsors_High-class_Dubai_World_Cup_Carnival_(8490930005).jpg)

Men wearing the [kandurah](/wiki/Thawb "Thawb") and the traditional [ghotrah](/wiki/Keffiyeh "Keffiyeh"), held in place by an [egal](/wiki/Agal_(accessory) "Agal (accessory)")

The Emirati attire is typical of several countries in the Arabian Peninsula. Women usually wear the "[abaya](/wiki/Abaya "Abaya")", a long black robe, with a [hijab](/wiki/Hijab "Hijab") (a headscarf that covers the neck and part of the head, all of the hair and the ears). Some women may wear a [niqab](/wiki/Niq%C4%81b "Niqāb") that covers the mouth and nose, leaving only the eyes exposed. Men wear the "[kandurah](/wiki/Thawb "Thawb")" also referred to as "dishdasha" or even "thawb" (long white robe) and the headscarf ([ghotrah](/wiki/Keffiyeh "Keffiyeh")). The traditional [ghutrah](/wiki/Ghutrah "Ghutrah") in the UAE is white and held in place by an accessory called "[egal](/wiki/Agal_(accessory) "Agal (accessory)")", which resembles a black cord. Younger Emiratis prefer to wear red-and-white ghutrah and tie them around their heads like turbans.[[293]](#cite_note-294)

The dress code is never compulsory; many people wear Western or other Eastern clothing. However, prohibitions on "indecent clothing" or revealing too much skin are aspects of the UAE to which Dubai's visitors are expected to conform and are encoded in Dubai's criminal law.[[294]](#cite_note-Criminal_Law_of_Dubai-295) The UAE has enforced decency regulations in most public places, aside from waterparks, beaches, clubs, and bars.[[295]](#cite_note-296)

## Education

Main article: [Education in Dubai](/wiki/Education_in_Dubai "Education in Dubai")

See also: [List of universities and colleges in Dubai](/wiki/List_of_universities_and_colleges_in_Dubai "List of universities and colleges in Dubai")

The school system in Dubai follows the United Arab Emirates' system. As of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dubai&action=edit), there are 79 public schools run by the Ministry of Education that serve Emiratis and expatriate Arab people, as well as 207 private schools.[[296]](#cite_note-297) The medium of instruction in public schools is Arabic with emphasis on English as a second language, while most of the private schools use English as their medium of instruction. Currently, only the Swiss International Scientific School in Dubai claims to offer parallel streams in different languages – bilingual English/French or English/German.[[297]](#cite_note-298) Most private schools cater to one or more expatriate communities.[[298]](#cite_note-299)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/UOWD_Campus.jpg/250px-UOWD_Campus.jpg)](/wiki/File:UOWD_Campus.jpg)

[University of Wollongong in Dubai](/wiki/University_of_Wollongong_in_Dubai "University of Wollongong in Dubai")

Some 36 schools offer international education using one or more of the four International Baccalaureate Programmes for students aged 3–19.[[299]](#cite_note-300) Currently, 15 schools[[300]](#cite_note-301) have introduced the IB Career-related Programme that can be combined with a vocational qualification such as a BTEC.[[301]](#cite_note-302)

While there are more UK-curriculum-based schools in Dubai than any other curriculum, more students attend Indian-curriculum schools, which tend to be considerably larger and cost less to attend. There are 34 Indian-curriculum schools in the emirate,[[302]](#cite_note-303) most of which offer the CBSE, and just a handful the Indian Certificate of Secondary Education (ICSE) Indian syllabus. Examples of Indian-curriculum schools include [IHS](/wiki/The_Indian_High_School,_Dubai "The Indian High School, Dubai"), [DPS](/wiki/Delhi_Private_School,_Dubai "Delhi Private School, Dubai"), and DMHS. There are a small number of Pakistani schools in Dubai offering the FBISE curriculum for expatriate children.

A total of 18 schools offer British primary education through age 11.[[303]](#cite_note-304) There are 64 schools that offer a variation of a UK curriculum style secondary education,[[304]](#cite_note-305) either a pure GCSE and A-Level offering, or increasingly I/GCSE up to 16, and then the IB Diploma post-16. Currently, no school in the UAE offers IB or A-level courses at age 16, but several schools have said they will offer them in the future. British style eleven-to-eighteen secondary schools offering General Certificate of Secondary Education and A-Levels include [Dubai College](/wiki/Dubai_College "Dubai College"), [Dubai British School](/wiki/Dubai_British_School "Dubai British School"), and [English Language School Pvt.](/wiki/English_Language_School,_Dubai "English Language School, Dubai") Some schools, such as The [American School of Dubai](/wiki/American_School_of_Dubai "American School of Dubai"), also offer the curriculum of the United States.[[305]](#cite_note-schlist-306)

Dubai has a very active education regulator, the KHDA, which is best known for its school ratings, but actually has a wide mandate[[306]](#cite_note-307) when it comes to school improvement in the emirate. A total of 17 schools are currently rated Outstanding (2020), and a further 40 are rated Very Good. Parents in general rate schools highly.[[307]](#cite_note-308)

The top 10 largest universities by student numbers in Dubai according to the [KHDA](/wiki/Knowledge_and_Human_Development_Authority "Knowledge and Human Development Authority") (2024)[[308]](#cite_note-:8-309) are [Middlesex University Dubai](/wiki/Middlesex_University_Dubai "Middlesex University Dubai"), [Heriot-Watt University Dubai](/wiki/Heriot-Watt_University_Dubai "Heriot-Watt University Dubai"), [University of Wollongong in Dubai](/wiki/University_of_Wollongong_in_Dubai "University of Wollongong in Dubai"), [Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Dubai](/wiki/Manipal_Academy_of_Higher_Education,_Dubai "Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Dubai"), [American University in Dubai](/wiki/American_University_in_Dubai "American University in Dubai"), [S P Jain School of Global Management](/wiki/S_P_Jain_School_of_Global_Management "S P Jain School of Global Management"), [Rochester Institute of Technology of Dubai](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology_of_Dubai "Rochester Institute of Technology of Dubai"), [Amity University Dubai](/wiki/Amity_University_Dubai "Amity University Dubai"), [University of Birmingham Dubai](/wiki/University_of_Birmingham "University of Birmingham"), and [Birla Institute of Technology and Science Pilani Dubai](/wiki/Birla_Institute_of_Technology_and_Science,_Pilani_%E2%80%93_Dubai_Campus "Birla Institute of Technology and Science, Pilani – Dubai Campus"). They offer courses in all major subjects, with the most popular subject areas being Business, followed by Information Technology, then Engineering.[[308]](#cite_note-:8-309) Most Universities in Dubai are located in the two Educational Zones ([Knowledge Village or Academic City](/wiki/Knowledge_village "Knowledge village")). In 2013, Synergy University Dubai Campus[[309]](#cite_note-310) opened its campus in [Jumeirah Lakes Towers](/wiki/Jumeirah_Lakes_Towers "Jumeirah Lakes Towers") being a first University in Dubai to be located outside of Educational Zones (Knowledge Village or Academic City).[[310]](#cite_note-311)

Only 4 Dubai Universities are present in the QS World University Rankings 2024/2025.[[311]](#cite_note-312) To allow the assessment of more UAE Universities, the Ministry of Education launched The National Higher Education Institutions Classification Framework[[312]](#cite_note-:9-313) which classifies UAE Universities based on their research and teaching. Of the top 10 largest Dubai universities [University of Wollongong in Dubai](/wiki/University_of_Wollongong_in_Dubai "University of Wollongong in Dubai"), [American University in Dubai](/wiki/American_University_in_Dubai "American University in Dubai"), and [Rochester Institute of Technology of Dubai](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology_of_Dubai "Rochester Institute of Technology of Dubai"), were all classified as "Very Good" within The National Higher Education Institutions Classification Framework.[[312]](#cite_note-:9-313)

## Healthcare

Main articles: [Dubai Health Authority](/wiki/Dubai_Health_Authority "Dubai Health Authority") and [List of hospitals in Dubai](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Dubai "List of hospitals in Dubai")

See also: [Dubai Camel Hospital](/wiki/Dubai_Camel_Hospital "Dubai Camel Hospital")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Dubai_Hospital.jpg/250px-Dubai_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Hospital.jpg)

The [Dubai Hospital](/wiki/Dubai_Hospital "Dubai Hospital")

Healthcare in Dubai can be divided into two different sectors: public and private. Each Emirate can set healthcare standards in accordance with its internal laws, though the standards and regulations rarely differ significantly. Public hospitals in Dubai were first established in the late 1950s and continued to expand alongside public health initiatives. There are now 28 hospitals in Dubai, six public and 22 private, with three more major hospitals scheduled to be built by 2025.[[313]](#cite_note-314)

By the end of 2012, there were also a total of 1,348 medical clinics, 97% of which were operated privately.[[314]](#cite_note-315) In 2015, Dubai phased in mandatory health insurance for all inhabitants, thereby leading to increased demand for medical services.[[315]](#cite_note-316)

Dubai Hospital is a [public hospital](/wiki/Public_hospital "Public hospital") in Dubai and is part of [Dubai Department of Health and Medical Services](/wiki/Dubai_Health_Authority "Dubai Health Authority"). Although the decision to construct Dubai Hospital was made in 1977, it did not begin admitting patients until March 1983.[[316]](#cite_note-317) The [hospital](/wiki/Hospital "Hospital") consists of 14 stories, with the lower two for Accident & Emergency and outpatients, and the upper ten floors.

* [![Morocco](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Flag_of_Morocco.svg/40px-Flag_of_Morocco.svg.png)](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca"), Morocco
